docs.title: http_server
docs.text:
Receive JSON data via HTTP POST requests. This input starts an HTTP server that accepts JSON objects or arrays and feeds them into the processing pipeline.

BasicCustom PathFull ConfigurationWith Basic AuthWith Bearer AuthWith Path ParametersMultiple Path ParametersWith TLSTLS with mTLSWith Retry 
input:
  http_server:
    port: 8080

input:
  http_server:
    address: "0.0.0.0"
    port: 9000
    path: "/events"

input:
  http_server:
    address: "127.0.0.1"
    port: 8080
    path: "/ingest"
    max_body_size: 5242880
    acknowledgment: true
    cors_enabled: true

input:
  http_server:
    port: 8080
    path: "/events"
    auth:
      type: basic
      username: admin
      password: secret123

input:
  http_server:
    port: 8080
    path: "/events"
    auth:
      type: bearer
      token: "my-api-token"

input:
  http_server:
    port: 8080
    path: "/ingest/:logtype"

input:
  http_server:
    port: 8080
    path: "/ingest/:source/:logtype"

input:
  http_server:
    port: 8443
    path: "/events"
    tls:
      cert: "/etc/fiddler/server.crt"
      key: "/etc/fiddler/server.key"

input:
  http_server:
    port: 8443
    tls:
      cert: "/etc/fiddler/server.crt"
      key: "/etc/fiddler/server.key"
      ca: "/etc/fiddler/ca.crt"
      client_auth: "required"

input:
  retry:
    max_retries: 3
    initial_wait: "1s"
    backoff: "exponential"
  http_server:
    port: 8080

docs.title: address
docs.text:
IP address to bind the server to.

Type: string Required: false Default: "0.0.0.0"

docs.title: port
docs.text:
Port number for the HTTP server.

Type: integer Required: false Default: 8080

docs.title: path
docs.text:
URL path for the ingestion endpoint. Supports path parameters using :name syntax (e.g. /ingest/:logtype). Path parameter values are added to each message's metadata.

Type: string Required: false Default: "/ingest"

Must start with /.

docs.title: max_body_size
docs.text:
Maximum request body size in bytes.

Type: integer Required: false Default: 10485760 (10 MB)

Requests exceeding this limit receive a 413 Payload Too Large response.

docs.title: acknowledgment
docs.text:
Wait for processing completion before responding.

Type: boolean Required: false Default: true

When true, the server waits for the message to be fully processed before sending the HTTP response. When false, it responds immediately after accepting the message.

docs.title: cors_enabled
docs.text:
Enable Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) headers.

Type: boolean Required: false Default: false

When enabled, allows requests from any origin with any method and headers.

docs.title: auth
docs.text:
Authentication for incoming requests. When configured, all requests to the ingestion endpoint must include valid credentials. The health endpoint (/health) remains unauthenticated.

Type: object Required: false

docs.title: auth.type
docs.text:
Authentication type.

Type: string Required: true (when auth is present)

Value Description basic HTTP Basic authentication bearer Bearer token authentication

docs.title: auth.username
docs.text:
Username for basic authentication.

Type: string Required: Required when auth.type is "basic"

docs.title: auth.password
docs.text:
Password for basic authentication.

Type: string Required: Required when auth.type is "basic"

docs.title: auth.token
docs.text:
Token for bearer authentication.

Type: string Required: Required when auth.type is "bearer"

Unauthenticated requests receive a 401 Unauthorized response: 

{"error": "Unauthorized"}

docs.title: tls
docs.text:
TLS configuration for HTTPS. When present, the server accepts HTTPS connections instead of plain HTTP.

!!! note "Feature Flag Required" TLS support requires both the http_server and tls features to be enabled when building fiddler. 

cargo build --features http_server,tls

Type: object Required: false

Each string field (cert, key, ca) accepts either a file path or inline PEM content. If the value starts with -----BEGIN, it is treated as inline PEM.

docs.title: tls.cert
docs.text:
Server certificate — file path or inline PEM.

Type: string Required: true (when tls is present)

docs.title: tls.key
docs.text:
Server private key — file path or inline PEM.

Type: string Required: true (when tls is present)

docs.title: tls.ca
docs.text:
CA certificate for verifying client certificates — file path or inline PEM.

Type: string Required: Required when tls.client_auth is not "none"

docs.title: tls.client_auth
docs.text:
Client certificate authentication mode.

Type: string Required: false Default: "none"

Value Description none No client certificate required optional Client certificate is requested but not required required Client must present a valid certificate signed by the CA

When set to "optional" or "required", tls.ca must also be provided.

docs.title: retry
docs.text:
Retry policy for failed reads. When present, the runtime retries failed reads with backoff.

Type: object Required: false

Field Type Default Description max_retries integer 3 Maximum retry attempts initial_wait string "1s" Wait before first retry max_wait string "30s" Maximum wait cap backoff string "exponential" Strategy: constant, linear, or exponential

docs.title: POST {path}
docs.text:
The main ingestion endpoint accepts JSON data.

Request: - Content-Type: application/json - Body: JSON object or array of objects

Response: - 200 OK: Message(s) accepted/processed - 401 Unauthorized: Missing or invalid credentials (when auth is configured) - 400 Bad Request: Invalid JSON - 413 Payload Too Large: Body exceeds max size - 500 Internal Server Error: Processing failed

Success Response: 

{
  "accepted": 2,
  "processed": 2
}

Partial Failure Response: 

{
  "accepted": 3,
  "processed": 2,
  "errors": ["Processing failed for message 1: ..."]
}

docs.title: GET /health
docs.text:
Health check endpoint.

Response: 

{"status": "healthy"}

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. The server binds to the configured address and port
  2. POST requests to the configured path are parsed as JSON
  3. Single objects become one message; arrays become multiple messages
  4. Messages are fed into the pipeline (optionally waiting for acknowledgment)
  5. HTTP response indicates success/failure

docs.title: Basic Event Ingestion
docs.text:
input:
  http_server:
    port: 8080
    path: "/events"

processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let data = parse_json(this);
        data = set(data, "received_at", now());
        this = bytes(str(data));

output:
  clickhouse:
    url: "http://localhost:8123"
    table: "events"

Send data: 

curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/events \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"event": "click", "user_id": 123}'

docs.title: Batch Ingestion
docs.text:
input:
  http_server:
    port: 8080
    path: "/batch"
    max_body_size: 52428800  # 50 MB

output:
  kafka:
    brokers: ["kafka:9092"]
    topic: "events"

Send array: 

curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/batch \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '[{"id": 1}, {"id": 2}, {"id": 3}]'

docs.title: Fire-and-Forget Mode
docs.text:
input:
  http_server:
    port: 8080
    acknowledgment: false  # Respond immediately

output:
  redis:
    url: "redis://localhost:6379"
    mode: "list"
    key: "events"

Useful when low latency is more important than confirmation.

docs.title: With CORS for Browser Clients
docs.text:
input:
  http_server:
    port: 8080
    path: "/api/events"
    cors_enabled: true

processors:
  - filter:
      condition: "event_type != null"

output:
  stdout: {}

docs.title: Localhost Only
docs.text:
input:
  http_server:
    address: "127.0.0.1"  # Only accept local connections
    port: 8080

output:
  stdout: {}

docs.title: HTTPS Server
docs.text:
input:
  http_server:
    port: 8443
    path: "/events"
    tls:
      cert: "/etc/fiddler/server.crt"
      key: "/etc/fiddler/server.key"

output:
  stdout: {}

Send data: 

curl -X POST https://localhost:8443/events \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  --cacert /etc/fiddler/ca.crt \
  -d '{"event": "click"}'

docs.title: Path Parameter Routing
docs.text:
Use path parameters to classify incoming data by URL segment. Each parameter is added to the message metadata.

input:
  http_server:
    port: 8080
    path: "/ingest/:source/:logtype"

processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let data = parse_json(this);
        let meta = metadata();
        data = set(data, "source", meta.source);
        data = set(data, "logtype", meta.logtype);
        this = bytes(str(data));

output:
  stdout: {}

Send data: 

# metadata: { "source": "firewall", "logtype": "network" }
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/ingest/firewall/network \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"event": "connection", "ip": "10.0.0.1"}'

docs.title: Webhook Receiver
docs.text:
input:
  http_server:
    port: 9000
    path: "/webhook"
    acknowledgment: true

processors:
  - transform:
      mappings:
        - source: "data.object"
          target: "payload"
        - source: "type"
          target: "event_type"

output:
  amqp:
    url: "amqp://localhost"
    exchange: "webhooks"

docs.title: Single Object
docs.text:
{"event": "click", "user_id": 123}

Produces one message.

docs.title: Array of Objects
docs.text:
[
  {"event": "click", "user_id": 123},
  {"event": "view", "user_id": 456}
]

Produces two messages (processed independently).

docs.title: Invalid Formats
docs.text:
• Non-JSON body: 400 Bad Request
  • Array with non-object elements: Elements are skipped
  • Primitive values: 400 Bad Request

docs.title: acknowledgment: true (default)
docs.text:
Client ──POST──► Server ──► Pipeline ──► Output
  ▲                                         │
  └─────────── 200 OK ◄────────────────────┘
  • Client waits for full processing
  • Errors are reported in the response
  • Higher latency but guaranteed delivery

docs.title: acknowledgment: false
docs.text:
Client ──POST──► Server ──► Pipeline ──► Output
  ▲               │
  └── 200 OK ◄────┘
  • Client gets immediate response
  • Processing continues in background
  • Lower latency but no error feedback

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text: Error HTTP Status Cause Unauthorized 401 Missing or invalid credentials (when auth is configured) Invalid JSON 400 Malformed JSON in request body Payload Too Large 413 Body exceeds max_body_size Processing Failed 500 Pipeline returned error (with acknowledgment: true)

docs.title: Performance Considerations
docs.text:
• Max body size: Set appropriately for your use case
  • Acknowledgment: Disable for lower latency if errors can be tolerated
  • Connection handling: Uses Axum with efficient async I/O
  • TLS: Adds per-connection handshake overhead; use when security is required

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • http output - Send data to HTTP endpoints

docs.title: mqtt
docs.text:
Subscribe to MQTT topics and receive messages. This input connects to an MQTT broker and subscribes to one or more topics using the MQTT 3.1.1 protocol.

BasicWith AuthenticationMultiple TopicsWith Retry 
input:
  mqtt:
    broker: "tcp://localhost:1883"
    topics:
      - "sensors/+"

input:
  mqtt:
    broker: "tcp://broker.example.com:1883"
    topics:
      - "events/#"
      - "logs/+"
    username: "user"
    password: "secret"
    qos: 2

input:
  mqtt:
    broker: "tcp://localhost:1883"
    client_id: "fiddler-subscriber"
    topics:
      - "home/+/temperature"
      - "home/+/humidity"
      - "alerts/#"
    qos: 1

input:
  retry:
    max_retries: 3
    initial_wait: "1s"
    backoff: "exponential"
  mqtt:
    broker: "tcp://localhost:1883"
    topics:
      - "sensors/+"

docs.title: broker
docs.text:
The MQTT broker URL.

Type: string Required: true

Format: tcp://host[:port] or mqtt://host[:port]

Default port is 1883 if not specified.

docs.title: topics
docs.text:
List of MQTT topic patterns to subscribe to.

Type: array[string] Required: true

Supports MQTT wildcards: - + matches a single topic level - # matches any number of levels (must be last character)

docs.title: client_id
docs.text:
Client identifier sent to the broker.

Type: string Required: false Default: Auto-generated UUID (fiddler_<uuid>)

Must be unique across all clients connected to the broker.

docs.title: qos
docs.text:
Quality of Service level for subscriptions.

Type: integer Required: false Default: 1

Value Description 0 At most once - fire and forget 1 At least once - acknowledged delivery 2 Exactly once - assured delivery

docs.title: username
docs.text:
Username for broker authentication.

Type: string Required: false

docs.title: password
docs.text:
Password for broker authentication.

Type: string Required: false

docs.title: keep_alive_secs
docs.text:
Keep-alive interval in seconds.

Type: integer Required: false Default: 60

The client sends ping requests to maintain the connection when idle.

docs.title: retry
docs.text:
Retry policy for failed reads. When present, the runtime retries failed reads with backoff.

Type: object Required: false

Field Type Default Description max_retries integer 3 Maximum retry attempts initial_wait string "1s" Wait before first retry max_wait string "30s" Maximum wait cap backoff string "exponential" Strategy: constant, linear, or exponential

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. The input connects to the MQTT broker
  2. It subscribes to all configured topics with the specified QoS
  3. Messages are received asynchronously and fed into the pipeline
  4. Automatic reconnection occurs on connection failures

docs.title: Single-Level Wildcard (+)
docs.text:
Matches exactly one topic level:

topics:
  - "sensors/+/temperature"  # Matches sensors/room1/temperature, sensors/room2/temperature
  - "home/+/+/status"        # Matches home/floor1/room1/status

docs.title: Multi-Level Wildcard (#)
docs.text:
Matches any number of levels (must be at the end):

topics:
  - "sensors/#"              # Matches sensors/temp, sensors/room/temp, etc.
  - "home/floor1/#"          # Matches all topics under home/floor1/

docs.title: IoT Sensor Data
docs.text:
input:
  mqtt:
    broker: "tcp://localhost:1883"
    topics:
      - "sensors/+/temperature"
      - "sensors/+/humidity"
    qos: 1

processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let data = parse_json(this);
        data = set(data, "received_at", now());
        this = bytes(str(data));

output:
  clickhouse:
    url: "http://localhost:8123"
    table: "sensor_readings"

docs.title: Secure Connection with Auth
docs.text:
input:
  mqtt:
    broker: "tcp://mqtt.example.com:1883"
    client_id: "fiddler-production"
    topics:
      - "events/#"
    qos: 2
    username: "fiddler"
    password: "${MQTT_PASSWORD}"
    keep_alive_secs: 30

output:
  kafka:
    brokers: ["kafka:9092"]
    topic: "mqtt-events"

docs.title: Home Automation
docs.text:
input:
  mqtt:
    broker: "tcp://homeassistant.local:1883"
    topics:
      - "homeassistant/sensor/+/state"
      - "homeassistant/binary_sensor/+/state"
      - "homeassistant/switch/+/state"

processors:
  - filter:
      condition: "state != 'unavailable'"

output:
  stdout: {}

docs.title: All Messages (Development)
docs.text:
input:
  mqtt:
    broker: "tcp://localhost:1883"
    topics:
      - "#"  # Subscribe to everything
    qos: 0  # Fire and forget for debugging

output:
  stdout: {}

docs.title: Quality of Service Levels
docs.text:
QoS Delivery Duplicates Use Case 0 At most once No Telemetry, non-critical data 1 At least once Possible Most applications 2 Exactly once No Critical data, transactions

Higher QoS levels add latency due to additional handshakes.

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
• Connection failures: Automatic reconnection with 1-second backoff
  • Subscribe failures: Logged per topic
  • Broker disconnection: Automatic reconnection

docs.title: Broker URL Format
docs.text:
tcp://host:port    # Standard MQTT (port 1883)
mqtt://host:port   # Alias for tcp://
host:port          # Protocol prefix optional
host               # Uses default port 1883

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • mqtt output - Publish messages to MQTT topics

docs.title: redis
docs.text:
Consume data from Redis using list operations (BLPOP/BRPOP), Pub/Sub subscriptions, or streams with consumer groups (XREADGROUP). This input supports queue-like consumption from lists, real-time message streaming via Pub/Sub, and at-least-once delivery with acknowledgment via Redis Streams.

List ModePub/Sub ModeStream ModeStream with Auto-ClaimPattern SubscribeWith Retry 
input:
  redis:
    url: "redis://localhost:6379"
    mode: "list"
    keys:
      - "input_queue"

input:
  redis:
    url: "redis://localhost:6379"
    mode: "pubsub"
    channels:
      - "events"
      - "notifications"

input:
  redis:
    url: "redis://localhost:6379"
    mode: "stream"
    streams:
      - "my_stream"
    consumer_group: "my_group"
    consumer_name: "worker-1"

input:
  redis:
    url: "redis://localhost:6379"
    mode: "stream"
    streams:
      - "my_stream"
    consumer_group: "my_group"
    stream_read_count: 100
    block_ms: 5000
    auto_claim:
      enabled: true
      idle_ms: 30000
      interval_ms: 10000
      batch_size: 100
    create_group: true

input:
  redis:
    url: "redis://localhost:6379"
    mode: "pubsub"
    channels:
      - "events.*"
      - "logs.*"
    use_patterns: true

input:
  retry:
    max_retries: 3
    initial_wait: "1s"
    backoff: "exponential"
  redis:
    url: "redis://localhost:6379"
    mode: "list"
    keys:
      - "input_queue"

docs.title: url
docs.text:
Redis connection URL.

Type: string Required: true

Format: redis://[username:password@]host[:port][/db]

docs.title: mode
docs.text:
Operation mode for consuming data.

Type: string Required: false Default: "list"

Value Description list Pop messages from Redis lists using BLPOP/BRPOP pubsub Subscribe to channels for real-time messages stream Read from streams using consumer groups (XREADGROUP) with at-least-once delivery

docs.title: keys
docs.text:
List key names to pop from (list mode only).

Type: array[string] Required: Required for list mode

Multiple keys are supported; the first key with data is popped.

docs.title: channels
docs.text:
Channel names or patterns to subscribe to (pubsub mode only).

Type: array[string] Required: Required for pubsub mode

docs.title: list_command
docs.text:
The blocking list operation to use.

Type: string Required: false Default: "brpop"

Value Description brpop Pop from the tail (FIFO queue behavior) blpop Pop from the head (LIFO stack behavior)

docs.title: timeout
docs.text:
Blocking timeout in seconds for list operations.

Type: integer Required: false Default: 1

docs.title: streams
docs.text:
Stream names to read from (stream mode only).

Type: array[string] Required: Required for stream mode

docs.title: consumer_group
docs.text:
Consumer group name for stream mode.

Type: string Required: Required for stream mode

docs.title: consumer_name
docs.text:
Consumer name within the group.

Type: string Required: false Default: hostname:pid

Defaults to hostname:pid if not specified. Supports Handlebars templating (e.g. "{{HOSTNAME}}:{{PID}}").

docs.title: stream_read_count
docs.text:
Maximum entries per XREADGROUP call.

Type: integer Required: false Default: 100

docs.title: block_ms
docs.text:
How long XREADGROUP blocks waiting for new messages, in milliseconds.

Type: integer Required: false Default: 5000

docs.title: auto_claim
docs.text:
Controls periodic reclamation of idle pending messages from dead consumers using XAUTOCLAIM (requires Redis 6.2+).

Type: object Required: false

Field Type Default Description enabled boolean true Enable auto-claim background task idle_ms integer 30000 Minimum idle time (ms) before a message is eligible for reclamation interval_ms integer 10000 How often the auto-claim task runs (ms) batch_size integer 100 Maximum messages to reclaim per cycle

Set idle_ms high enough to avoid stealing in-flight messages from healthy consumers.

docs.title: create_group
docs.text:
Automatically create the consumer group (and stream via MKSTREAM) on startup.

Type: boolean Required: false Default: true

If false and the group or stream doesn't exist, startup fails with a validation error.

docs.title: use_patterns
docs.text:
Enable pattern matching for Pub/Sub subscriptions.

Type: boolean Required: false Default: false

When enabled, channel names are treated as patterns (uses PSUBSCRIBE).

docs.title: retry
docs.text:
Retry policy for failed reads. When present, the runtime retries failed reads with backoff.

Type: object Required: false

Field Type Default Description max_retries integer 3 Maximum retry attempts initial_wait string "1s" Wait before first retry max_wait string "30s" Maximum wait cap backoff string "exponential" Strategy: constant, linear, or exponential

docs.title: List Mode
docs.text:
1. Connects to Redis using the provided URL
  2. Executes BRPOP/BLPOP on the specified keys with timeout
  3. Returns the first available message from any key
  4. Repeats indefinitely until shutdown

docs.title: Stream Mode
docs.text:
1. Connects to Redis and creates the consumer group if create_group: true
  2. Reads any pending messages for this consumer first (crash recovery)
  3. Enters the main read loop using XREADGROUP with BLOCK
  4. When a message is processed by the output, XACK is sent to acknowledge it
  5. Unacknowledged messages remain in the pending entry list (PEL) and are reprocessed on restart
  6. If auto-claim is enabled, a background task periodically runs XAUTOCLAIM to reclaim idle messages from dead consumers

docs.title: Pub/Sub Mode
docs.text:
1. Connects to Redis and creates a Pub/Sub connection
  2. Subscribes to all specified channels (or patterns if use_patterns: true)
  3. Messages are received asynchronously as they're published
  4. Automatic reconnection on connection failures

docs.title: Simple Queue Processing
docs.text:
input:
  redis:
    url: "redis://localhost:6379"
    mode: "list"
    keys:
      - "job_queue"

processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let job = parse_json(this);
        // Process job...
        this = bytes(str(job));

output:
  stdout: {}

docs.title: Priority Queue with Multiple Keys
docs.text:
input:
  redis:
    url: "redis://localhost:6379"
    mode: "list"
    keys:
      - "high_priority"
      - "normal_priority"
      - "low_priority"
    list_command: "brpop"
    timeout: 5

output:
  http:
    url: "https://api.example.com/process"

Keys are checked in order; first non-empty key wins.

docs.title: Real-Time Event Streaming
docs.text:
input:
  redis:
    url: "redis://localhost:6379"
    mode: "pubsub"
    channels:
      - "events"
      - "notifications"

output:
  kafka:
    brokers: ["kafka:9092"]
    topic: "redis-events"

docs.title: Pattern-Based Subscriptions
docs.text:
input:
  redis:
    url: "redis://localhost:6379"
    mode: "pubsub"
    channels:
      - "sensor:*:temperature"
      - "sensor:*:humidity"
    use_patterns: true

processors:
  - transform:
      mappings:
        - source: "value"
          target: "reading"
        - source: "timestamp"
          target: "ts"

output:
  clickhouse:
    url: "http://localhost:8123"
    table: "sensor_data"

docs.title: Stream Consumer Group
docs.text:
input:
  redis:
    url: "redis://localhost:6379"
    mode: "stream"
    streams:
      - "order_events"
    consumer_group: "order_processors"
    block_ms: 5000
    auto_claim:
      enabled: true
      idle_ms: 30000
    create_group: true

processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let order = parse_json(this);
        // Process order...
        this = bytes(str(order));

output:
  http:
    url: "https://api.example.com/orders"

Messages are acknowledged after successful output processing. If a consumer crashes, pending messages are automatically reclaimed by other consumers via XAUTOCLAIM.

docs.title: Stack Processing (LIFO)
docs.text:
input:
  redis:
    url: "redis://localhost:6379"
    mode: "list"
    keys:
      - "task_stack"
    list_command: "blpop"  # Pop from head = LIFO

output:
  stdout: {}

docs.title: With Authentication
docs.text:
input:
  redis:
    url: "redis://default:mypassword@redis.example.com:6379/0"
    mode: "list"
    keys:
      - "secure_queue"

output:
  stdout: {}

docs.title: List vs Pub/Sub vs Stream
docs.text: Feature List Mode Pub/Sub Mode Stream Mode Persistence Until consumed Ephemeral Persistent with trimming Multiple consumers Load distributed All receive all Consumer groups distribute load Ordering FIFO/LIFO guaranteed Not guaranteed Guaranteed within stream Backpressure Natural (list grows) Messages dropped if slow Natural (stream grows) Replay Not possible (consumed) Not possible Possible (stream persists) Acknowledgment Implicit (pop removes) None Explicit (XACK) Dead consumer recovery N/A N/A Auto-claim from PEL

docs.title: When to Use List Mode
docs.text:
• Simple job queues and task processing
  • Load distribution across workers
  • No need for message replay

docs.title: When to Use Pub/Sub
docs.text:
• Real-time notifications
  • Event broadcasting
  • Fan-out patterns

docs.title: When to Use Stream Mode
docs.text:
• At-least-once delivery guarantees
  • Consumer group coordination across instances
  • Dead consumer recovery without message loss
  • Message replay and audit trails

docs.title: Pattern Syntax (Pub/Sub)
docs.text:
When use_patterns: true:

Pattern Matches sensor:* sensor:1, sensor:room1, etc. logs:*:error logs:app:error, logs:db:error event:? event:1, event:a (single char) data:[abc] data:a, data:b, data:c

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
• Connection failures: Automatic reconnection with backoff
  • List timeout: Returns to blocking wait
  • Pub/Sub disconnection: Stream ends, reconnection attempted

docs.title: Redis URL Format
docs.text:
redis://localhost                    # Localhost, default port
redis://host:6380                    # Custom port
redis://user:pass@host:6379          # With authentication
redis://host:6379/1                  # Database 1
redis://:password@host:6379/0        # Password only (no username)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • redis output - Push data to Redis lists, publish to channels, or write to streams

docs.title: stdin
docs.text:
Read data from stdin by lines and forward to the pipeline

RequiredWith Retry 
input:
    stdin: {}

input:
  retry:
    max_retries: 3
    initial_wait: "1s"
    backoff: "exponential"
  stdin: {}

docs.title: retry
docs.text:
Retry policy for failed reads. When present, the runtime retries failed reads with backoff.

Type: object Required: false

Field Type Default Description max_retries integer 3 Maximum retry attempts initial_wait string "1s" Wait before first retry max_wait string "30s" Maximum wait cap backoff string "exponential" Strategy: constant, linear, or exponential

docs.title: syslog
docs.text:
Receive log data via syslog protocol over UDP, TCP, or TLS. Supports both RFC 5424 (IETF) and RFC 3164 (BSD) message formats. Compatible with rsyslog, syslog-ng, and other standard syslog forwarders.

UDP (default)TCPTLSTLS (inline PEM)Full ConfigurationWith Retry 
input:
  syslog:
    port: 1514

input:
  syslog:
    port: 1514
    transport: "tcp"

input:
  syslog:
    port: 6514
    transport: "tls"
    tls:
      cert: "/etc/fiddler/server.crt"
      key: "/etc/fiddler/server.key"

input:
  syslog:
    port: 6514
    transport: "tls"
    tls:
      cert: |
        -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
        MIIBxTCCAW...
        -----END CERTIFICATE-----
      key: |
        -----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----
        MIIEvQ...
        -----END PRIVATE KEY-----

input:
  syslog:
    address: "0.0.0.0"
    port: 1514
    transport: "tcp"
    framing: "auto"
    max_connections: 512
    connection_timeout: 60
    allow_cidrs:
      - "10.0.0.0/8"
      - "192.168.0.0/16"
    max_message_size: 65536
    socket_receive_buffer: 4194304
    channel_buffer_size: 10000

input:
  retry:
    max_retries: 3
    initial_wait: "1s"
    backoff: "exponential"
  syslog:
    port: 1514

docs.title: address
docs.text:
IP address to bind the listener to.

Type: string Required: false Default: "0.0.0.0"

docs.title: port
docs.text:
Port number for the syslog listener.

Type: integer Required: false Default: 1514

The default is 1514 (unprivileged). To use the standard syslog port 514, the process requires CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE or root privileges on Linux.

docs.title: transport
docs.text:
Network transport protocol.

Type: string Required: false Default: "udp"

Value Description udp Stateless, best-effort delivery. Most common for syslog. tcp Reliable, connection-oriented. Supports framed messages. tls TCP with TLS encryption (RFC 5425). Requires certificate configuration.

docs.title: framing
docs.text:
TCP/TLS message framing method per RFC 6587.

Type: string Required: false Default: "auto"

Value Description auto Auto-detect per connection from the first byte octet_counting Length-prefixed: <length> <message> — robust, no ambiguity newline Newline-delimited — legacy default used by rsyslog

Only applies to TCP and TLS transports. When set to auto, the first byte of each connection determines the framing: a digit selects octet-counting, < selects newline-delimited.

docs.title: max_connections
docs.text:
Maximum number of concurrent TCP/TLS connections.

Type: integer Required: false Default: 512

Connections beyond this limit are rejected with a warning log. Minimum value: 1.

docs.title: connection_timeout
docs.text:
Idle connection timeout in seconds.

Type: integer Required: false Default: 60

TCP/TLS connections that send no data within this period are closed. Protects against slowloris-style attacks. Minimum value: 1.

docs.title: allow_cidrs
docs.text:
IP/CIDR allowlist for source addresses.

Type: array[string] Required: false Default: [] (allow all)

When non-empty, only connections from IPs matching at least one CIDR are accepted. Others are rejected with a warning log.

allow_cidrs:
  - "10.0.0.0/8"
  - "192.168.1.0/24"
  - "172.16.0.0/12"

docs.title: tls
docs.text:
TLS configuration block. Required when transport is "tls".

Type: object Required: Required when transport is "tls"

Each string field (cert, key, ca) accepts either a file path or inline PEM content. If the value starts with -----BEGIN, it is treated as inline PEM; otherwise it is treated as a file path.

docs.title: tls.cert
docs.text:
Server certificate — file path or inline PEM.

Type: string Required: true (when tls is present)

docs.title: tls.key
docs.text:
Server private key — file path or inline PEM.

Type: string Required: true (when tls is present)

The module warns at startup if the key file permissions are more open than 0600.

docs.title: tls.ca
docs.text:
CA certificate for verifying client certificates — file path or inline PEM.

Type: string Required: Required when tls.client_auth is not "none"

docs.title: tls.client_auth
docs.text:
Client certificate authentication mode.

Type: string Required: false Default: "none"

Value Description none No client certificate required optional Client certificate is requested but not required required Client must present a valid certificate signed by the CA

When set to "optional" or "required", tls.ca must also be provided.

docs.title: max_message_size
docs.text:
Maximum syslog message size in bytes.

Type: integer Required: false Default: 65536 (64 KB)

Messages exceeding this limit are rejected. Valid range: 256 to 16,777,216 (16 MB).

For octet-counting framing, the declared length is checked before allocating memory. For newline framing, reads are bounded to this size.

docs.title: socket_receive_buffer
docs.text:
UDP socket receive buffer size hint in bytes.

Type: integer Required: false Default: 4194304 (4 MB)

Passed to the OS as SO_RCVBUF. The actual buffer size may be limited by OS settings (net.core.rmem_max on Linux). Only applies to UDP transport.

docs.title: channel_buffer_size
docs.text:
Internal channel capacity between the listener and the pipeline.

Type: integer Required: false Default: 10000

When the channel is full, incoming messages are dropped with a warning log.

docs.title: retry
docs.text:
Retry policy for failed reads. When present, the runtime retries failed reads with backoff.

Type: object Required: false

Field Type Default Description max_retries integer 3 Maximum retry attempts initial_wait string "1s" Wait before first retry max_wait string "30s" Maximum wait cap backoff string "exponential" Strategy: constant, linear, or exponential

docs.title: Message Output
docs.text: The parsed syslog message body is placed in Message.bytes. Syslog envelope fields are stored in Message.metadata.

docs.title: Metadata Fields
docs.text:
Field Type Description Example syslog_facility string Facility name "kern", "local0" syslog_facility_code integer Facility numeric code 0, 16 syslog_severity string Severity name "err", "info" syslog_severity_code integer Severity numeric code 3, 6 syslog_timestamp string Timestamp (RFC 3339 when available) "2024-01-15T10:30:00+00:00" syslog_hostname string Source hostname or IP "myhost" syslog_appname string Application name "sshd" syslog_procid string Process ID "1234" syslog_msgid string Message type identifier (RFC 5424) "ID47" syslog_version integer Syslog protocol version (RFC 5424) 1 syslog_structured_data map Parsed structured data elements (RFC 5424) {"exampleSDID@32473": {"iut": "3"}} syslog_format string Detected message format "rfc5424" or "rfc3164" syslog_source_ip string Sender IP address "10.0.0.1" syslog_raw string Original raw syslog line Full syslog frame

Fields with NIL values (- in RFC 5424) are omitted from metadata.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. The listener binds to the configured address and port
  2. For UDP: datagrams are received and parsed individually
  3. For TCP/TLS: connections are accepted and framed messages are read
  4. Each syslog message is parsed using both RFC 5424 and RFC 3164 formats (best match)
  5. The message body goes into Message.bytes; envelope fields go into Message.metadata
  6. Messages are sent to the processing pipeline

docs.title: Receive from rsyslog (UDP)
docs.text:
input:
  syslog:
    port: 1514

processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let msg = str(this);
        let data = {};
        data = set(data, "message", msg);
        this = bytes(str(data));

output:
  stdout: {}

rsyslog configuration: 

*.* @fiddler-host:1514

docs.title: Receive from rsyslog (TCP)
docs.text:
input:
  syslog:
    port: 1514
    transport: "tcp"
    framing: "auto"

output:
  elasticsearch:
    urls:
      - "http://localhost:9200"
    index: "syslog-%Y.%m.%d"

rsyslog configuration: 

*.* @@fiddler-host:1514

docs.title: TLS with Mutual Authentication
docs.text:
input:
  syslog:
    port: 6514
    transport: "tls"
    tls:
      cert: "/etc/fiddler/server.crt"
      key: "/etc/fiddler/server.key"
      ca: "/etc/fiddler/ca.crt"
      client_auth: "required"

output:
  clickhouse:
    url: "http://localhost:8123"
    table: "secure_logs"

docs.title: Restricted Source IPs
docs.text:
input:
  syslog:
    port: 1514
    transport: "tcp"
    allow_cidrs:
      - "10.0.0.0/8"
      - "192.168.1.0/24"
    max_connections: 256

output:
  stdout: {}

Only accepts connections from 10.0.0.0/8 and 192.168.1.0/24.

docs.title: High-Volume UDP with Increased Buffer
docs.text:
input:
  syslog:
    port: 1514
    transport: "udp"
    socket_receive_buffer: 16777216  # 16 MB
    max_message_size: 8192
    channel_buffer_size: 50000

output:
  file:
    path: "/var/log/fiddler/syslog.log"

For high-volume environments, also increase the OS limit: 

sysctl -w net.core.rmem_max=16777216

docs.title: Filter by Severity
docs.text:
input:
  syslog:
    port: 1514

processors:
  - filter:
      condition: "syslog_severity_code <= `4`"

output:
  stdout: {}

Only forwards messages with severity warning or higher (lower numeric code = higher severity).

docs.title: Route by Facility
docs.text:
input:
  syslog:
    port: 1514
    transport: "tcp"

processors:
  - switch:
      - condition: "syslog_facility == 'auth' || syslog_facility == 'authpriv'"
        processors:
          - fiddlerscript:
              code: |
                let data = {};
                data = set(data, "type", "security");
                data = set(data, "message", str(this));
                this = bytes(str(data));

output:
  stdout: {}

docs.title: Transport Comparison
docs.text: Feature UDP TCP TLS Reliability Best-effort Reliable Reliable Ordering Not guaranteed Per-connection Per-connection Encryption None None TLS 1.2+ Source IP trust Spoofable Trustworthy Trustworthy RFC RFC 5426 RFC 6587 RFC 5425 Default port 514 / 1514 514 / 1514 6514 Connection overhead None Per-connection Per-connection + handshake

docs.title: When to Use UDP
docs.text:
• High-volume logging where occasional message loss is acceptable
  • Legacy environments where TCP syslog is not supported
  • Low-latency requirements

docs.title: When to Use TCP
docs.text:
• Reliable delivery is required
  • Messages may exceed the typical UDP MTU (~1500 bytes)
  • Firewall environments that block UDP

docs.title: When to Use TLS
docs.text:
• Logs traverse untrusted networks
  • Compliance requirements mandate encryption
  • Client authentication is needed (mTLS)

docs.title: TCP Framing
docs.text: TCP syslog uses framing to separate messages on a stream. Two methods are defined in RFC 6587:

docs.title: Octet-Counting
docs.text:
<length> <message>

The message is preceded by its byte length as ASCII digits followed by a space. This is unambiguous and handles messages containing newlines.

Example: 70 <165>1 2024-01-15T10:30:00Z myhost myapp 1234 ID47 - Hello world

docs.title: Newline-Delimited
docs.text:
<message>\n

Messages are separated by newline characters. This is the legacy default used by rsyslog. Messages cannot contain embedded newlines.

docs.title: Auto-Detection
docs.text:
When framing: "auto" (default), the first byte of each connection determines the method:

  • Digit (0-9): octet-counting
  • <: newline-delimited (syslog messages start with <priority>)
  • Other: connection rejected

docs.title: Security Considerations
docs.text:
• UDP source IPs are spoofable — do not rely on syslog_source_ip from UDP for authentication or authorization
  • All metadata fields are untrusted — they come from the network and may contain malicious content
  • Use allow_cidrs to restrict which hosts can send logs
  • Use TLS when logs traverse untrusted networks
  • Use tls.client_auth: "required" for mutual TLS to authenticate senders
  • max_connections and connection_timeout protect against connection exhaustion attacks
  • max_message_size prevents memory exhaustion from oversized messages
  • Privileged ports — port 514 requires CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE or root: 
    sudo setcap 'cap_net_bind_service=+ep' /usr/local/bin/fiddler-cli

docs.title: RFC 5424 (IETF)
docs.text:
<priority>version timestamp hostname appname procid msgid [structured-data] message

Example: 

<165>1 2024-01-15T10:30:00Z myhost myapp 1234 ID47 [exampleSDID@32473 iut="3"] Application started

docs.title: RFC 3164 (BSD)
docs.text:
<priority>timestamp hostname tag: message

Example: 

<34>Oct 11 22:14:15 mymachine sshd[1234]: Failed password for invalid user

The parser automatically detects the format and sets the syslog_format metadata field accordingly.

docs.title: rsyslog
docs.text:
# UDP forwarding
*.* @fiddler-host:1514

# TCP forwarding
*.* @@fiddler-host:1514

# TCP with octet-counting
$ActionSendStreamDriver gtls
$ActionSendStreamDriverMode 1
*.* @@fiddler-host:6514;RSYSLOG_SyslogProtocol23Format

docs.title: syslog-ng
docs.text:
destination d_fiddler {
    syslog("fiddler-host"
        port(1514)
        transport("tcp")
    );
};

log {
    source(s_sys);
    destination(d_fiddler);
};

docs.title: systemd-journald
docs.text:
# /etc/systemd/journal-remote.conf
[Remote]
URL=fiddler-host:1514

Or use logger for testing: 

logger -n fiddler-host -P 1514 --tcp "Test message from logger"

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text: Condition Behavior Channel full Message dropped, warning logged Oversized message Message rejected, warning logged Connection limit reached Connection rejected, warning logged Idle timeout Connection closed, warning logged Invalid framing Connection closed, warning logged TLS handshake failure Connection closed, warning logged Non-allowed source IP Connection/datagram rejected, warning logged

docs.title: Performance Considerations
docs.text:
• socket_receive_buffer: Increase for high-volume UDP to reduce kernel drops. Also increase the OS limit (net.core.rmem_max).
  • channel_buffer_size: Increase to absorb bursts without dropping messages.
  • max_connections: Set based on the expected number of concurrent senders.
  • UDP vs TCP: UDP has lower overhead but no delivery guarantees. TCP adds per-connection state.
  • num_threads (pipeline-level): Controls parallelism for processing received messages.

docs.title: zeromq
docs.text:
Receive messages using ZeroMQ sockets. This input supports PULL and SUB socket types for building distributed messaging patterns.

Pull SocketSubscribe SocketConnect to MultipleWith Retry 
input:
  zeromq:
    socket_type: "pull"
    bind: "tcp://*:5555"

input:
  zeromq:
    socket_type: "sub"
    connect:
      - "tcp://publisher:5555"
    subscribe:
      - "events"
      - "logs"

input:
  zeromq:
    socket_type: "pull"
    connect:
      - "tcp://worker1:5555"
      - "tcp://worker2:5555"
      - "tcp://worker3:5555"

input:
  retry:
    max_retries: 3
    initial_wait: "1s"
    backoff: "exponential"
  zeromq:
    socket_type: "pull"
    bind: "tcp://*:5555"

docs.title: socket_type
docs.text:
The type of ZeroMQ socket to create.

Type: string Required: true

Value Description pull Receive messages from PUSH sockets (pipeline pattern) sub Subscribe to topics from PUB sockets (pub/sub pattern)

docs.title: bind
docs.text:
Address to bind the socket to.

Type: string Required: false (but either bind or connect must be specified)

Format: <transport>://<address>

docs.title: connect
docs.text:
Addresses to connect to.

Type: array[string] Required: false (but either bind or connect must be specified)

Multiple addresses can be specified to connect to several endpoints.

docs.title: subscribe
docs.text:
Topic filters for SUB sockets.

Type: array[string] Required: Required for sub socket type

Use "" (empty string) to receive all messages.

docs.title: retry
docs.text:
Retry policy for failed reads. When present, the runtime retries failed reads with backoff.

Type: object Required: false

Field Type Default Description max_retries integer 3 Maximum retry attempts initial_wait string "1s" Wait before first retry max_wait string "30s" Maximum wait cap backoff string "exponential" Strategy: constant, linear, or exponential

docs.title: PULL Socket
docs.text:
1. Creates a PULL socket and binds/connects to the specified addresses
  2. Messages are received from connected PUSH sockets
  3. Messages are load-balanced across multiple PUSH senders
  4. Fair-queuing ensures even distribution

docs.title: SUB Socket
docs.text:
1. Creates a SUB socket and binds/connects to the specified addresses
  2. Subscribes to the specified topic filters
  3. Messages matching any filter are received
  4. Topic matching is prefix-based

docs.title: Pipeline (PUSH/PULL)
docs.text:
Distributes work across multiple workers:

         ┌──► [PULL Worker 1]
[PUSH] ──┼──► [PULL Worker 2]
         └──► [PULL Worker 3]

# Worker configuration
input:
  zeromq:
    socket_type: "pull"
    connect:
      - "tcp://dispatcher:5555"

docs.title: Pub/Sub
docs.text:
Broadcasts messages to all subscribers:

              ┌──► [SUB Client 1]
[PUB] ───────┼──► [SUB Client 2]
              └──► [SUB Client 3]

# Subscriber configuration
input:
  zeromq:
    socket_type: "sub"
    connect:
      - "tcp://publisher:5555"
    subscribe:
      - "events"

docs.title: Work Distribution Pipeline
docs.text:
# Collector receiving from multiple producers
input:
  zeromq:
    socket_type: "pull"
    bind: "tcp://*:5555"

processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let data = parse_json(this);
        data = set(data, "collector_ts", now());
        this = bytes(str(data));

output:
  clickhouse:
    url: "http://localhost:8123"
    table: "events"

docs.title: Subscribe to All Messages
docs.text:
input:
  zeromq:
    socket_type: "sub"
    connect:
      - "tcp://publisher:5555"
    subscribe:
      - ""  # Empty string = all messages

output:
  stdout: {}

docs.title: Multi-Topic Subscription
docs.text:
input:
  zeromq:
    socket_type: "sub"
    connect:
      - "tcp://events.example.com:5555"
    subscribe:
      - "order:"
      - "payment:"
      - "shipment:"

processors:
  - filter:
      condition: "type != null"

output:
  kafka:
    brokers: ["kafka:9092"]
    topic: "events"

docs.title: Connect to Multiple Publishers
docs.text:
input:
  zeromq:
    socket_type: "sub"
    connect:
      - "tcp://server1:5555"
      - "tcp://server2:5555"
      - "tcp://server3:5555"
    subscribe:
      - "metrics"

output:
  stdout: {}

docs.title: Bind vs Connect
docs.text:
Both SUB and PULL sockets can either bind or connect:

# Publisher binds, subscriber connects (common)
input:
  zeromq:
    socket_type: "sub"
    connect:
      - "tcp://publisher:5555"
    subscribe:
      - ""

# Subscriber binds, publisher connects (reverse)
input:
  zeromq:
    socket_type: "sub"
    bind: "tcp://*:5556"
    subscribe:
      - ""

docs.title: Transport Types
docs.text: Transport Format Description TCP tcp://host:port Network communication IPC ipc:///path Inter-process (Unix sockets) inproc inproc://name In-process threads

docs.title: TCP Examples
docs.text:
bind: "tcp://*:5555"              # Bind to all interfaces
bind: "tcp://127.0.0.1:5555"      # Localhost only
connect:
  - "tcp://remote:5555"           # Connect to remote host

docs.title: IPC Examples
docs.text:
bind: "ipc:///tmp/fiddler.sock"
connect:
  - "ipc:///tmp/producer.sock"

docs.title: Topic Matching (SUB)
docs.text:
Topic filtering uses prefix matching:

Subscribe Matches "" All messages "order" "order", "order:new", "order:update" "order:" "order:new", "order:update" (not "order") "error" "error", "error.critical", "errors"

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
• Bind failures: Error returned during startup
  • Connect failures: Logged, connection retried automatically
  • Receive errors: Logged, continues listening

docs.title: Performance Considerations
docs.text:
• High-water mark: ZeroMQ buffers messages; backpressure is handled internally
  • Multiple connections: Fair-queuing across all connected sockets
  • Message framing: ZeroMQ handles message boundaries automatically

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• zeromq output - Send messages using ZeroMQ sockets
  • ZeroMQ Guide - Comprehensive ZeroMQ patterns

docs.title: Metrics
docs.text:
Metrics provide observability into the fiddler runtime by exposing statistics about message processing. Metrics configuration is optional - if not provided, metrics are disabled with zero overhead.

metrics:
  prometheus: {}

docs.title: Available Metrics
docs.text:
The following metrics are tracked and exposed by all metrics backends:

Metric Type Description total_received Counter Total messages received from input total_completed Counter Messages successfully processed through all outputs total_filtered Counter Messages intentionally filtered/dropped by processors total_process_errors Counter Messages that encountered processing errors total_output_errors Counter Messages that encountered output errors streams_started Counter Number of streams started streams_completed Counter Number of streams completed duplicates_rejected Counter Duplicate messages rejected stale_entries_removed Counter Stale entries cleaned up from state tracker in_flight Gauge Current number of messages being processed throughput_per_sec Gauge Current throughput in messages per second input_bytes Counter Total bytes received from input output_bytes Counter Total bytes written to output bytes_per_sec Gauge Current throughput in bytes per second latency_avg_ms Gauge Average message processing latency in milliseconds latency_min_ms Gauge Minimum message processing latency in milliseconds latency_max_ms Gauge Maximum message processing latency in milliseconds

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Metrics are configured at the top level of the pipeline configuration:

label: My Pipeline
metrics:
  prometheus: {}
input:
  stdin: {}
processors:
  - noop: {}
output:
  stdout: {}

docs.title: Available Backends
docs.text: Backend Description Feature Flag clickhouse Send metrics to ClickHouse for time-series storage clickhouse cloudwatch AWS CloudWatch metrics aws prometheus Prometheus-compatible metrics endpoint prometheus stdout Output metrics as JSON to stdout -

docs.title: clickhouse
docs.text:
Send pipeline metrics to ClickHouse for time-series storage and analysis.

!!! note "Feature Flag Required" This metrics backend requires the clickhouse feature to be enabled when building fiddler. 

cargo build --features clickhouse

BasicFull 
metrics:
  clickhouse:
    url: "http://localhost:8123"

metrics:
  interval: 60
  clickhouse:
    url: "http://localhost:8123"
    database: fiddler
    table: metrics
    username: default
    password: secret
    include:
      - total_received
      - total_completed
      - throughput_per_sec
    exclude:
      - stale_entries_removed
    dimensions:
      - name: environment
        value: production
      - name: pipeline
        value: main
    batch_size: 100
    flush_interval_ms: 5000
    create_table: true
    ttl_days: 30

docs.title: url
docs.text: ClickHouse HTTP interface URL. Type: string Required: true

docs.title: database
docs.text: Target database name for metrics. Type: string Default: "fiddler" Required: false

docs.title: table
docs.text: Target table name for metrics. Type: string Default: "metrics" Required: false

docs.title: username
docs.text: Username for authentication. Type: string Required: false

docs.title: password
docs.text: Password for authentication. Type: string Required: false

docs.title: include
docs.text: List of metric names to include. If not specified, all metrics are included. Type: array[string] Required: false

docs.title: exclude
docs.text: List of metric names to exclude. Applied after include filter. Type: array[string] Required: false

docs.title: dimensions
docs.text: Additional dimension columns added to all metric rows. Useful for identifying pipelines or environments. Type: array Required: false

docs.title: Dimension Object
docs.text: Field Type Description name string Dimension column name value string Constant value for all rows

docs.title: batch_size
docs.text: Number of metric entries to batch before sending to ClickHouse. Type: integer Default: 100 Required: false

docs.title: flush_interval_ms
docs.text: Maximum time in milliseconds between flushes. Type: integer Default: 5000 Required: false

docs.title: create_table
docs.text: Automatically create the metrics table if it doesn't exist. Type: boolean Default: true Required: false

docs.title: ttl_days
docs.text: Time-to-live for metrics in days. Set to 0 to disable automatic expiration. Type: integer Default: 0 Required: false

docs.title: Available Metrics
docs.text:
The following metrics can be tracked:

Metric Name Type Description total_received counter Total messages received from input total_completed counter Messages successfully processed total_filtered counter Messages intentionally filtered/dropped by processors total_process_errors counter Messages with processing errors total_output_errors counter Messages with output errors streams_started counter Streams initiated streams_completed counter Streams finished duplicates_rejected counter Duplicate messages rejected stale_entries_removed counter Stale entries cleaned up in_flight gauge Messages currently being processed throughput_per_sec gauge Processing throughput (messages/sec) input_bytes counter Total input bytes received output_bytes counter Total output bytes written bytes_per_sec gauge Throughput in bytes per second latency_avg_ms gauge Average message processing latency in milliseconds latency_min_ms gauge Minimum message processing latency in milliseconds latency_max_ms gauge Maximum message processing latency in milliseconds

docs.title: Table Schema
docs.text:
When create_table: true, the following table is created:

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS fiddler.metrics (
    timestamp DateTime64(3),
    metric_name String,
    metric_value Float64
    [, dimension1 String, dimension2 String, ...]
) ENGINE = MergeTree()
ORDER BY (timestamp, metric_name)
[TTL toDateTime(timestamp) + INTERVAL n DAY]

docs.title: With Dimensions Example
docs.text:
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS fiddler.metrics (
    timestamp DateTime64(3),
    metric_name String,
    metric_value Float64,
    environment String,
    pipeline String
) ENGINE = MergeTree()
ORDER BY (timestamp, metric_name)
TTL toDateTime(timestamp) + INTERVAL 30 DAY

docs.title: Include Filter
docs.text:
Only track specific metrics: 

metrics:
  clickhouse:
    url: "http://localhost:8123"
    include:
      - total_received
      - total_completed
      - throughput_per_sec

docs.title: Exclude Filter
docs.text:
Track all metrics except specific ones: 

metrics:
  clickhouse:
    url: "http://localhost:8123"
    exclude:
      - stale_entries_removed
      - duplicates_rejected

docs.title: Combined Filters
docs.text:
Include is applied first, then exclude: 

metrics:
  clickhouse:
    url: "http://localhost:8123"
    include:
      - total_received
      - total_completed
      - total_process_errors
      - total_output_errors
    exclude:
      - total_output_errors  # Will be excluded

docs.title: Recent Throughput
docs.text:
SELECT
    toStartOfMinute(timestamp) AS minute,
    avg(metric_value) AS avg_throughput
FROM fiddler.metrics
WHERE metric_name = 'throughput_per_sec'
  AND timestamp > now() - INTERVAL 1 HOUR
GROUP BY minute
ORDER BY minute

docs.title: Error Rate Over Time
docs.text:
SELECT
    toStartOfHour(timestamp) AS hour,
    sumIf(metric_value, metric_name = 'total_process_errors') AS errors,
    sumIf(metric_value, metric_name = 'total_completed') AS completed,
    errors / (errors + completed) * 100 AS error_rate_pct
FROM fiddler.metrics
WHERE timestamp > now() - INTERVAL 24 HOUR
GROUP BY hour
ORDER BY hour

docs.title: By Pipeline (with dimensions)
docs.text:
SELECT
    pipeline,
    max(metric_value) AS max_throughput
FROM fiddler.metrics
WHERE metric_name = 'throughput_per_sec'
  AND timestamp > now() - INTERVAL 1 HOUR
GROUP BY pipeline

docs.title: Connection Settings
docs.text: Setting Value Max idle connections per host 2 Idle timeout 90 seconds Request timeout 30 seconds Connection timeout 10 seconds Channel buffer 1000 entries

docs.title: Full Pipeline Example
docs.text:
label: Production Pipeline
input:
  http_server:
    port: 8080
processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      source: |
        root = this
output:
  clickhouse:
    url: "http://clickhouse:8123"
    database: events
    table: raw_events
metrics:
  interval: 30
  clickhouse:
    url: "http://clickhouse:8123"
    database: observability
    table: pipeline_metrics
    dimensions:
      - name: pipeline
        value: production_ingestion
      - name: environment
        value: prod
    include:
      - total_received
      - total_completed
      - throughput_per_sec
      - in_flight
    batch_size: 50
    flush_interval_ms: 3000
    ttl_days: 90

docs.title: Buffer Overflow
docs.text:
If metrics are generated faster than they can be sent, a warning is logged: 

WARN Metrics channel full, dropping metric. Consider increasing batch_size or reducing flush_interval_ms

Solutions: - Increase batch_size for higher throughput - Decrease flush_interval_ms for more frequent flushes - Use include filter to reduce metric volume

docs.title: TTL for Storage Management
docs.text:
Use ttl_days to automatically expire old metrics: 

metrics:
  clickhouse:
    url: "http://localhost:8123"
    ttl_days: 30  # Keep 30 days of metrics

docs.title: Graceful Shutdown
docs.text: On shutdown, remaining buffered metrics are flushed with a 5-second timeout to ensure data is not lost.

docs.title: cloudwatch
docs.text:
Send metrics to AWS CloudWatch for centralized monitoring and alerting.

!!! note "Feature Flag Required" This metrics backend requires the aws feature to be enabled when building fiddler. 

cargo build --features aws

BasicWith NamespaceWith FilteringFull Example 
metrics:
  cloudwatch: {}

metrics:
  cloudwatch:
    namespace: "MyApplication"
    region: "us-east-1"

metrics:
  cloudwatch:
    namespace: "Fiddler"
    include:
      - total_received
      - total_completed
      - throughput_per_sec
    exclude:
      - stale_entries_removed

label: Production Pipeline
metrics:
  cloudwatch:
    namespace: "Fiddler/Production"
    region: "us-west-2"
    credentials:
      access_key_id: "AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE"
      secret_access_key: "wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY"
    dimensions:
      - name: Environment
        value: production
      - name: Service
        value: data-pipeline
    include:
      - total_received
      - total_completed
      - total_process_errors
      - total_output_errors
      - in_flight
      - throughput_per_sec
input:
  stdin: {}
processors:
  - noop: {}
output:
  stdout: {}

docs.title: Fields
docs.text: Field Type Default Description namespace string "Fiddler" CloudWatch namespace for organizing metrics region string AWS default AWS region (uses default provider chain if not set) credentials object AWS default Explicit AWS credentials (uses default provider chain if not set) include array all metrics List of metric names to include exclude array none List of metric names to exclude dimensions array none Additional dimensions to add to all metrics

docs.title: Credentials Object
docs.text: Field Type Required Description access_key_id string Yes AWS access key ID secret_access_key string Yes AWS secret access key session_token string No AWS session token (for temporary credentials)

docs.title: Dimension Object
docs.text: Field Type Required Description name string Yes Dimension name value string Yes Dimension value

docs.title: Available Metrics
docs.text:
The following metrics can be included or excluded:

Metric Name Type Description total_received Count Total messages received from input total_completed Count Messages successfully processed through all outputs total_filtered Count Messages intentionally filtered/dropped by processors total_process_errors Count Messages that encountered processing errors total_output_errors Count Messages that encountered output errors streams_started Count Number of streams started streams_completed Count Number of streams completed duplicates_rejected Count Duplicate messages rejected stale_entries_removed Count Stale entries cleaned up from state tracker in_flight Count Current number of messages being processed throughput_per_sec Count/Second Current throughput in messages per second

docs.title: Metric Filtering
docs.text:
By default, all metrics are published to CloudWatch. You can control which metrics are sent using the include and exclude fields:

  • include: When specified, only the listed metrics are published. If empty or omitted, all metrics are included.
  • exclude: Metrics listed here are never published, even if they appear in include.

The exclude filter is applied after include, allowing you to include most metrics while excluding specific ones.

docs.title: Example: Only Performance Metrics
docs.text:
metrics:
  cloudwatch:
    include:
      - throughput_per_sec
      - in_flight

docs.title: Example: All Except Cleanup Metrics
docs.text:
metrics:
  cloudwatch:
    exclude:
      - stale_entries_removed
      - duplicates_rejected

docs.title: AWS Authentication
docs.text: The CloudWatch backend supports multiple authentication methods:

docs.title: 1. Default Provider Chain (Recommended)
docs.text:
When no credentials are specified, the AWS SDK uses its default credential provider chain:

  1. Environment variables (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY)
  2. Shared credentials file (~/.aws/credentials)
  3. IAM role (when running on EC2, ECS, Lambda, etc.)
metrics:
  cloudwatch:
    region: "us-east-1"

docs.title: 2. Explicit Credentials
docs.text:
For development or specific use cases, you can provide credentials directly:

metrics:
  cloudwatch:
    credentials:
      access_key_id: "AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE"
      secret_access_key: "wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY"

!!! warning "Security" Avoid committing credentials to version control. Use environment variables or IAM roles for production deployments.

docs.title: CloudWatch Dimensions
docs.text:
Dimensions allow you to categorize and filter metrics in CloudWatch. Common use cases include:

  • Distinguishing between environments (dev, staging, production)
  • Identifying specific services or pipelines
  • Grouping by region or availability zone
metrics:
  cloudwatch:
    dimensions:
      - name: Environment
        value: production
      - name: Pipeline
        value: logs-processor

All configured dimensions are attached to every metric published.

docs.title: IAM Permissions
docs.text:
The CloudWatch backend requires the following IAM permissions:

{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "cloudwatch:PutMetricData"
      ],
      "Resource": "*"
    }
  ]
}

You can restrict the Resource to specific namespaces using conditions:

{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": "cloudwatch:PutMetricData",
      "Resource": "*",
      "Condition": {
        "StringEquals": {
          "cloudwatch:namespace": "Fiddler"
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Performance Considerations
docs.text:
• Non-blocking: Metrics are published asynchronously using a background task
  • Buffered: A bounded channel (100 entries) prevents backpressure from affecting pipeline performance
  • Dropped on overflow: If the channel is full, metrics are dropped rather than blocking the pipeline

docs.title: Viewing Metrics in CloudWatch
docs.text:
1. Open the AWS CloudWatch console
  2. Navigate to Metrics > All metrics
  3. Select your namespace (default: "Fiddler")
  4. Browse or search for specific metric names

docs.title: Example CloudWatch Insights Query
docs.text:
SELECT AVG(throughput_per_sec), MAX(in_flight)
FROM SCHEMA("Fiddler", Environment, Pipeline)
WHERE Environment = 'production'
GROUP BY Pipeline
ORDER BY AVG(throughput_per_sec) DESC

docs.title: Metrics Not Appearing
docs.text:
1. Verify the aws feature is enabled in your build
  2. Check IAM permissions for cloudwatch:PutMetricData
  3. Confirm the correct region is configured
  4. Look for error logs indicating CloudWatch API failures

docs.title: High Latency
docs.text:
CloudWatch has API rate limits. If you're publishing many metrics frequently:

  1. Consider using the include filter to reduce metric count
  2. Increase the metrics interval in your pipeline configuration
  3. Use dimensions to aggregate related data points

docs.title: prometheus
docs.text:
Expose metrics via a Prometheus-compatible endpoint using the metrics crate.

!!! note "Feature Flag Required" This metrics backend requires the prometheus feature to be enabled when building fiddler. 

cargo build --features prometheus

BasicFull Example 
metrics:
  prometheus: {}

label: My Pipeline
metrics:
  prometheus: {}
input:
  stdin: {}
processors:
  - noop: {}
output:
  stdout: {}

docs.title: Exposed Metrics
docs.text: The following Prometheus metrics are exposed:

docs.title: Counters
docs.text: Metric Name Description fiddler_messages_received_total Total messages received from input fiddler_messages_completed_total Messages successfully processed through all outputs fiddler_messages_filtered_total Messages intentionally filtered/dropped by processors fiddler_messages_process_errors_total Messages that encountered processing errors fiddler_messages_output_errors_total Messages that encountered output errors fiddler_streams_started_total Number of streams started fiddler_streams_completed_total Number of streams completed fiddler_duplicates_rejected_total Duplicate messages rejected fiddler_stale_entries_removed_total Stale entries cleaned up fiddler_input_bytes_total Total bytes received from input fiddler_output_bytes_total Total bytes written to output

docs.title: Gauges
docs.text: Metric Name Description fiddler_messages_in_flight Current number of messages being processed fiddler_throughput_per_second Current throughput in messages per second fiddler_bytes_per_second Current throughput in bytes per second fiddler_latency_avg_ms Average message processing latency in milliseconds fiddler_latency_min_ms Minimum message processing latency in milliseconds fiddler_latency_max_ms Maximum message processing latency in milliseconds

docs.title: Scraping Metrics
docs.text: The Prometheus exporter uses the default configuration from metrics-exporter-prometheus. Metrics are typically available at http://localhost:9000/metrics (default port).

docs.title: Example Prometheus Configuration
docs.text:
scrape_configs:
  - job_name: 'fiddler'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['localhost:9000']

docs.title: Fields
docs.text:
Currently, the prometheus backend accepts an empty configuration object. Future versions may support additional configuration options such as:

  • Custom port binding
  • Custom endpoint path
  • Default labels

docs.title: Example Future Configuration (Not Yet Implemented)
docs.text:
metrics:
  prometheus:
    port: 9090
    path: /metrics
    labels:
      environment: production
      service: my-pipeline

docs.title: stdout
docs.text:
Output metrics as JSON to stdout for debugging and local development.

BasicPretty Print 
metrics:
  stdout: {}

metrics:
  interval: 60
  stdout:
    pretty: true

docs.title: pretty
docs.text: Enable pretty-printed JSON output with indentation. Type: boolean Default: false Required: false

docs.title: Standard Output (pretty: false)
docs.text: {"total_received":1000,"total_completed":995,"total_filtered":3,"total_process_errors":2,"total_output_errors":0,"streams_started":0,"streams_completed":0,"duplicates_rejected":0,"stale_entries_removed":0,"in_flight":5,"throughput_per_sec":123.45,"input_bytes":102400,"output_bytes":98304,"bytes_per_sec":9830.4,"latency_avg_ms":5.5,"latency_min_ms":1.2,"latency_max_ms":15.8}

  "total_output_errors": 0,
  "streams_started": 0,
