docs.text:
auth:
  type: "bearer"
  token: "your-api-token"

docs.title: timeout_secs
docs.text:
Request timeout in seconds.

Type: integer Required: false Default: 30

docs.title: batch
docs.text:
Batching configuration. When present, enables batch mode.

Type: object Required: false

Field Type Default Description size integer 500 Maximum messages per batch duration string "10s" Maximum time before flush max_batch_bytes integer 10485760 Maximum cumulative byte size per batch (default: 10MB) format string "ndjson" Batch payload format

docs.title: Batch Formats
docs.text: Format Description ndjson Newline-delimited JSON (one JSON per line) json_array JSON array of objects

docs.title: tls
docs.text:
TLS configuration for custom CA certificates and client certificates (mTLS).

Type: object Required: false

Each string field (ca, cert, key) accepts either a file path or inline PEM content. If the value starts with -----BEGIN, it is treated as inline PEM.

Field Type Default Description ca string — CA certificate for server verification cert string — Client certificate for mTLS key string — Client private key for mTLS skip_verify boolean false Skip server certificate verification

docs.title: retry
docs.text:
Retry policy for failed writes. When present, the runtime retries failed writes with backoff.

Type: object Required: false

Field Type Default Description max_retries integer 3 Maximum retry attempts initial_wait string "1s" Wait before first retry max_wait string "30s" Maximum wait cap backoff string "exponential" Strategy: constant, linear, or exponential

4xx responses are never retried (permanent failures). 5xx and connection errors are retried.

docs.title: Single Message Mode (no batch)
docs.text:
1. Each message is sent as an individual HTTP request
  2. The message bytes become the request body
  3. Response status is checked (non-2xx is an error)

docs.title: Batch Mode (with batch config)
docs.text:
1. Messages are buffered until batch size or duration is reached
  2. Messages are formatted according to the batch format
  3. A single HTTP request is sent with the batch payload
  4. Response status is checked

docs.title: Simple POST
docs.text:
input:
  kafka:
    brokers: ["kafka:9092"]
    topics: ["events"]

output:
  http:
    url: "https://api.example.com/events"
    headers:
      Content-Type: "application/json"

docs.title: With Bearer Token Auth
docs.text:
input:
  http_server:
    port: 8080

output:
  http:
    url: "https://api.example.com/ingest"
    auth:
      type: "bearer"
      token: "${API_TOKEN}"
    headers:
      Content-Type: "application/json"

docs.title: With Basic Auth
docs.text:
output:
  http:
    url: "https://api.example.com/data"
    auth:
      type: "basic"
      username: "service"
      password: "${API_PASSWORD}"

docs.title: Batched NDJSON
docs.text:
input:
  kinesis:
    stream_name: "events"

output:
  http:
    url: "https://api.example.com/bulk"
    batch:
      size: 1000
      duration: "10s"
      format: "ndjson"
    headers:
      Content-Type: "application/x-ndjson"

Request body: 

{"event":"click","user":1}
{"event":"view","user":2}
{"event":"click","user":3}

docs.title: Batched JSON Array
docs.text:
output:
  http:
    url: "https://api.example.com/batch"
    batch:
      size: 100
      duration: "5s"
      format: "json_array"
    headers:
      Content-Type: "application/json"

Request body: 

[
  {"event":"click","user":1},
  {"event":"view","user":2},
  {"event":"click","user":3}
]

docs.title: Webhook Delivery
docs.text:
input:
  amqp:
    url: "amqp://localhost"
    queue: "notifications"

output:
  http:
    url: "https://webhook.example.com/notify"
    method: "POST"
    headers:
      Content-Type: "application/json"
      X-Webhook-Secret: "${WEBHOOK_SECRET}"
    timeout_secs: 10

docs.title: PUT Method
docs.text:
output:
  http:
    url: "https://api.example.com/resource"
    method: "PUT"
    headers:
      Content-Type: "application/json"

docs.title: Batch Format Comparison
docs.text: Format Content-Type Use Case ndjson application/x-ndjson Streaming ingestion, log aggregators json_array application/json APIs expecting array payloads

docs.title: NDJSON Format
docs.text:
{"id":1,"name":"Alice"}
{"id":2,"name":"Bob"}
  • Each message on its own line
  • No wrapping array
  • Easy to stream and parse line-by-line

docs.title: JSON Array Format
docs.text:
[{"id":1,"name":"Alice"},{"id":2,"name":"Bob"}]
  • Valid JSON array
  • Messages are parsed and re-serialized
  • Compatible with standard JSON APIs

docs.title: Duration Syntax
docs.text:
The batch.duration field accepts duration strings:

Format Example Description Milliseconds 100ms 100 milliseconds Seconds 5s 5 seconds Minutes 2m 2 minutes Hours 1h 1 hour

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
• Connection failures: Error returned to pipeline
  • Non-2xx responses: Error with status code and body
  • Timeout: Error after configured timeout

docs.title: Response Handling
docs.text: Successful responses (2xx) are considered successful. Error responses include: - HTTP status code - Response body (for debugging)

docs.title: Performance Considerations
docs.text:
• Batching: Significantly reduces HTTP overhead for high-volume data
  • Connection pooling: Reuses connections to the same host
  • Timeout: Set appropriately for your endpoint's latency

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • http_server input - Receive data via HTTP

docs.title: mqtt
docs.text:
Publish messages to an MQTT topic. This output connects to an MQTT broker and publishes messages to a specified topic using the MQTT 3.1.1 protocol.

BasicWith QoS and RetainWith AuthenticationWith Retry 
output:
  mqtt:
    broker: "tcp://localhost:1883"
    topic: "events/processed"

output:
  mqtt:
    broker: "tcp://broker.example.com:1883"
    topic: "sensors/latest"
    qos: 2
    retain: true

output:
  mqtt:
    broker: "tcp://mqtt.example.com:1883"
    client_id: "fiddler-publisher"
    topic: "data/output"
    username: "user"
    password: "secret"

output:
  retry:
    max_retries: 5
    initial_wait: "2s"
    backoff: "exponential"
  mqtt:
    broker: "tcp://localhost:1883"
    topic: "events/processed"

docs.title: broker
docs.text:
The MQTT broker URL.

Type: string Required: true

Format: tcp://host[:port] or mqtt://host[:port]

Default port is 1883 if not specified.

docs.title: topic
docs.text:
The MQTT topic to publish messages to.

Type: string Required: true

Unlike input topics, output topics cannot contain wildcards.

docs.title: client_id
docs.text:
Client identifier sent to the broker.

Type: string Required: false Default: Auto-generated UUID (fiddler_<uuid>)

Must be unique across all clients connected to the broker.

docs.title: qos
docs.text:
Quality of Service level for published messages.

Type: integer Required: false Default: 1

Value Description 0 At most once - fire and forget 1 At least once - acknowledged delivery 2 Exactly once - assured delivery

docs.title: retain
docs.text:
When enabled, the broker stores the message and delivers it to new subscribers.

Type: boolean Required: false Default: false

Retained messages are useful for: - Last known state values - Configuration data - Status indicators

docs.title: username
docs.text:
Username for broker authentication.

Type: string Required: false

docs.title: password
docs.text:
Password for broker authentication.

Type: string Required: false

docs.title: retry
docs.text:
Retry policy for failed writes. When present, the runtime retries failed writes with backoff.

Type: object Required: false

Field Type Default Description max_retries integer 3 Maximum retry attempts initial_wait string "1s" Wait before first retry max_wait string "30s" Maximum wait cap backoff string "exponential" Strategy: constant, linear, or exponential

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. The output connects to the MQTT broker
  2. An event loop is spawned to handle acknowledgments and reconnections
  3. Each message is published to the configured topic
  4. The QoS level determines the delivery guarantee

docs.title: Basic Publishing
docs.text:
input:
  http_server:
    port: 8080

processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let data = parse_json(this);
        data = set(data, "processed", true);
        this = bytes(str(data));

output:
  mqtt:
    broker: "tcp://localhost:1883"
    topic: "events/processed"

docs.title: Sensor Data with Retain
docs.text:
input:
  mqtt:
    broker: "tcp://localhost:1883"
    topics:
      - "sensors/raw/+"

processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let data = parse_json(this);
        data = set(data, "timestamp", now());
        this = bytes(str(data));

output:
  mqtt:
    broker: "tcp://localhost:1883"
    topic: "sensors/processed"
    retain: true  # Last value available to new subscribers
    qos: 1

docs.title: Home Automation Command Relay
docs.text:
input:
  http_server:
    port: 9000
    path: "/command"

output:
  mqtt:
    broker: "tcp://homeassistant.local:1883"
    topic: "homeassistant/switch/relay/set"
    qos: 2  # Exactly once for commands

docs.title: Secure Publishing
docs.text:
input:
  kafka:
    brokers: ["kafka:9092"]
    topics: ["internal-events"]

output:
  mqtt:
    broker: "tcp://mqtt.example.com:1883"
    client_id: "fiddler-bridge"
    topic: "external/events"
    username: "bridge"
    password: "${MQTT_PASSWORD}"
    qos: 1

docs.title: Status Updates
docs.text:
input:
  stdin: {}

processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let status = #{"status": "online", "timestamp": now()};
        this = bytes(str(status));

output:
  mqtt:
    broker: "tcp://localhost:1883"
    topic: "services/fiddler/status"
    retain: true  # Persist status
    qos: 1

docs.title: Quality of Service Levels
docs.text: QoS Delivery Latency Use Case 0 At most once Lowest High-frequency telemetry 1 At least once Medium Most applications 2 Exactly once Highest Critical commands, transactions

docs.title: Retained Messages
docs.text:
When retain: true: - The broker stores the last message for the topic - New subscribers immediately receive the retained message - Sending an empty message clears the retained message

Use cases: - Device status (online/offline) - Current sensor values - Configuration state

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
• Connection failures: Automatic reconnection with backoff
  • Publish failures: Error returned to pipeline
  • Broker disconnection: Event loop handles reconnection

docs.title: Broker URL Format
docs.text:
tcp://host:port    # Standard MQTT (port 1883)
mqtt://host:port   # Alias for tcp://
host:port          # Protocol prefix optional
host               # Uses default port 1883

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • mqtt input - Subscribe to MQTT topics

docs.title: redis
docs.text:
Send data to Redis using list operations (LPUSH/RPUSH), Pub/Sub publishing, or stream appends (XADD). This output supports queue-like insertion into lists, real-time message broadcasting via Pub/Sub, and persistent stream writes with optional trimming.

List ModePub/Sub ModeStream ModeStream with TrimmingWith BatchingWith Retry 
output:
  redis:
    url: "redis://localhost:6379"
    mode: "list"
    key: "output_queue"

output:
  redis:
    url: "redis://localhost:6379"
    mode: "pubsub"
    channel: "events"

output:
  redis:
    url: "redis://localhost:6379"
    mode: "stream"
    stream: "my_stream"

output:
  redis:
    url: "redis://localhost:6379"
    mode: "stream"
    stream: "my_stream"
    max_len: 10000
    batch:
      size: 500
      duration: "10s"

output:
  redis:
    url: "redis://localhost:6379"
    mode: "list"
    key: "batch_queue"
    batch:
      size: 500
      duration: "5s"

output:
  retry:
    max_retries: 5
    initial_wait: "2s"
    backoff: "exponential"
  redis:
    url: "redis://localhost:6379"
    mode: "list"
    key: "output_queue"

docs.title: url
docs.text:
Redis connection URL.

Type: string Required: true

Format: redis://[username:password@]host[:port][/db]

docs.title: mode
docs.text:
Operation mode for sending data.

Type: string Required: false Default: "list"

Value Description list Push messages to a Redis list using LPUSH/RPUSH pubsub Publish messages to a channel stream Append messages to a stream using XADD

docs.title: key
docs.text:
List key name to push to (list mode only).

Type: string Required: Required for list mode

docs.title: channel
docs.text:
Channel name to publish to (pubsub mode only).

Type: string Required: Required for pubsub mode

docs.title: stream
docs.text:
Stream name to write to (stream mode only).

Type: string Required: Required for stream mode

docs.title: max_len
docs.text:
Approximate maximum stream length. When set, every XADD includes MAXLEN ~ <max_len> to cap stream size. Redis trims approximately — the actual length may exceed this value slightly.

Type: integer Required: false

docs.title: list_command
docs.text:
The list operation to use.

Type: string Required: false Default: "rpush"

Value Description rpush Push to the tail (queue behavior) lpush Push to the head (stack behavior)

docs.title: batch
docs.text:
Batching policy for grouping messages (list and stream modes).

Type: object Required: false

Field Type Description size integer Maximum messages per batch (default: 500) duration string Maximum time before flush (default: "10s") max_batch_bytes integer Maximum cumulative byte size per batch (default: 10MB)

Note: Batching is supported in list and stream modes. It is not supported in pubsub mode and will be ignored.

docs.title: retry
docs.text:
Retry policy for failed writes. When present, the runtime retries failed writes with backoff.

Type: object Required: false

Field Type Default Description max_retries integer 3 Maximum retry attempts initial_wait string "1s" Wait before first retry max_wait string "30s" Maximum wait cap backoff string "exponential" Strategy: constant, linear, or exponential

Authentication failures are never retried.

docs.title: List Mode
docs.text:
1. Messages are buffered according to the batch policy
  2. When a batch is ready, messages are pushed using Redis pipelining
  3. RPUSH adds to the tail (FIFO queue behavior)
  4. LPUSH adds to the head (LIFO stack behavior)

docs.title: Stream Mode
docs.text:
1. Messages are appended to the stream using XADD with auto-generated entry IDs
  2. Message bytes are stored in the data field; metadata entries are stored with meta: prefix
  3. If max_len is set, approximate trimming (MAXLEN ~) keeps the stream capped
  4. When batching is enabled, multiple XADD commands are pipelined in a single network round-trip

docs.title: Pub/Sub Mode
docs.text:
1. Each message is published individually to the channel
  2. Messages are delivered to all subscribed clients
  3. No persistence - undelivered messages are lost

docs.title: Simple Queue Output
docs.text:
input:
  http_server:
    port: 8080

processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let data = parse_json(this);
        data = set(data, "processed_at", now());
        this = bytes(str(data));

output:
  redis:
    url: "redis://localhost:6379"
    mode: "list"
    key: "processed_events"

docs.title: High-Throughput with Batching
docs.text:
input:
  kafka:
    brokers: ["kafka:9092"]
    topics: ["events"]

output:
  redis:
    url: "redis://localhost:6379"
    mode: "list"
    key: "event_queue"
    list_command: "rpush"
    batch:
      size: 1000
      duration: "5s"

docs.title: Stream with Trimming
docs.text:
input:
  kafka:
    brokers: ["kafka:9092"]
    topics: ["events"]

output:
  redis:
    url: "redis://localhost:6379"
    mode: "stream"
    stream: "event_stream"
    max_len: 100000
    batch:
      size: 500
      duration: "5s"

Stream entries persist and can be consumed by multiple consumer groups. The max_len keeps the stream from growing unbounded.

docs.title: Event Broadcasting
docs.text:
input:
  http_server:
    port: 8080
    path: "/broadcast"

output:
  redis:
    url: "redis://localhost:6379"
    mode: "pubsub"
    channel: "notifications"

docs.title: Stack (LIFO) Output
docs.text:
input:
  stdin: {}

output:
  redis:
    url: "redis://localhost:6379"
    mode: "list"
    key: "task_stack"
    list_command: "lpush"  # New items at head = LIFO

docs.title: Authenticated Connection
docs.text:
input:
  kinesis:
    stream_name: "events"

output:
  redis:
    url: "redis://default:secretpassword@redis.example.com:6379/0"
    mode: "list"
    key: "kinesis_events"
    batch:
      size: 500
      duration: "10s"

docs.title: Multi-Channel Publishing
docs.text:
For publishing to multiple channels, use multiple outputs:

input:
  http_server:
    port: 8080

processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let data = parse_json(this);
        this = bytes(str(data));

output:
  switch:
    cases:
      - check: "type == 'alert'"
        output:
          redis:
            url: "redis://localhost:6379"
            mode: "pubsub"
            channel: "alerts"
      - output:
          redis:
            url: "redis://localhost:6379"
            mode: "pubsub"
            channel: "events"

docs.title: List vs Pub/Sub vs Stream
docs.text: Feature List Mode Pub/Sub Mode Stream Mode Persistence Yes No Yes (with optional trimming) Batching Supported Not supported Supported Delivery Guaranteed Best effort Guaranteed Consumers One consumes each message All receive all Consumer groups distribute load Backpressure List grows Slow consumers miss messages Stream grows (trimmable) Replay Not possible Not possible Possible

docs.title: When to Use List Mode
docs.text:
• Simple job queues and task distribution
  • Buffering between services
  • No need for message replay

docs.title: When to Use Pub/Sub
docs.text:
• Real-time notifications
  • Event broadcasting
  • Fan-out patterns

docs.title: When to Use Stream Mode
docs.text:
• Durable event logs with consumer groups
  • Multiple independent consumer groups on the same data
  • Message replay and audit trails
  • At-least-once delivery when paired with stream input

docs.title: Batching with Pipelining
docs.text: In list and stream modes, batching uses Redis pipelining for efficiency: - Multiple RPUSH/LPUSH or XADD commands are sent in a single network round-trip - Reduces latency and increases throughput - Atomic per-batch (all or nothing)

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
• Connection failures: Automatic reconnection with connection manager
  • Write failures: Error returned to pipeline
  • Reconnection: Seamless recovery without data loss (for queued messages)

docs.title: Redis URL Format
docs.text:
redis://localhost                    # Localhost, default port
redis://host:6380                    # Custom port
redis://user:pass@host:6379          # With authentication
redis://host:6379/1                  # Database 1
redis://:password@host:6379/0        # Password only (no username)

docs.title: Performance Considerations
docs.text:
• Batch size: Larger batches improve throughput but increase latency
  • Pipelining: Automatically used in list mode for batches
  • Connection pooling: Managed internally

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • redis input - Pop data from Redis lists, subscribe to channels, or read from streams

docs.title: stdout
docs.text:
Emit process messages to stdout

RequiredWith Retry 
output:
    stdout: {}

output:
  retry:
    max_retries: 5
    initial_wait: "2s"
    backoff: "exponential"
  stdout: {}

docs.title: retry
docs.text:
Retry policy for failed writes. When present, the runtime retries failed writes with backoff.

Type: object Required: false

Field Type Default Description max_retries integer 3 Maximum retry attempts initial_wait string "1s" Wait before first retry max_wait string "30s" Maximum wait cap backoff string "exponential" Strategy: constant, linear, or exponential

docs.title: switch
docs.text:
Switch accepts an array of valid outputs and is expected to be utlized with the check output. Switch will attempt each provided output; while failing to the next provided the error is returned is ConditionalCheckfailed. Other encountered errors will be surfaced.

Required 
output:
  switch: []

docs.title: check
docs.text:
Required 
output:
  switch:
    - check:
        condition: '\"Hello World\" > `5`'
        output: 
          stdout: {}"

docs.title: condition
docs.text: Condition utilized for the execution of the output step utilizing jmespath syntax for evaluation. As such, this can only be utilized with JSON documents. Type: string Required: true

docs.title: output
docs.text: Valid fiddler output module Type: object Required: true

docs.title: retry
docs.text:
Retry policy for failed writes. When present, the runtime retries failed writes with backoff.

Type: object Required: false

Field Type Default Description max_retries integer 3 Maximum retry attempts initial_wait string "1s" Wait before first retry max_wait string "30s" Maximum wait cap backoff string "exponential" Strategy: constant, linear, or exponential

docs.title: zeromq
docs.text:
Send messages using ZeroMQ sockets. This output supports PUSH and PUB socket types for building distributed messaging patterns.

Push SocketPublish SocketConnect to MultipleWith Retry 
output:
  zeromq:
    socket_type: "push"
    connect:
      - "tcp://collector:5555"

output:
  zeromq:
    socket_type: "pub"
    bind: "tcp://*:5555"

output:
  zeromq:
    socket_type: "push"
    connect:
      - "tcp://worker1:5555"
      - "tcp://worker2:5555"
      - "tcp://worker3:5555"

output:
  retry:
    max_retries: 5
    initial_wait: "2s"
    backoff: "exponential"
  zeromq:
    socket_type: "push"
    connect:
      - "tcp://collector:5555"

docs.title: socket_type
docs.text:
The type of ZeroMQ socket to create.

Type: string Required: true

Value Description push Send messages to PULL sockets (pipeline pattern) pub Publish messages to SUB sockets (pub/sub pattern)

docs.title: bind
docs.text:
Address to bind the socket to.

Type: string Required: false (but either bind or connect must be specified)

Format: <transport>://<address>

docs.title: connect
docs.text:
Addresses to connect to.

Type: array[string] Required: false (but either bind or connect must be specified)

Multiple addresses can be specified to connect to several endpoints.

docs.title: retry
docs.text:
Retry policy for failed writes. When present, the runtime retries failed writes with backoff.

Type: object Required: false

Field Type Default Description max_retries integer 3 Maximum retry attempts initial_wait string "1s" Wait before first retry max_wait string "30s" Maximum wait cap backoff string "exponential" Strategy: constant, linear, or exponential

docs.title: PUSH Socket
docs.text:
1. Creates a PUSH socket and binds/connects to the specified addresses
  2. Messages are sent to connected PULL sockets
  3. Load-balancing distributes messages across multiple receivers
  4. Back-pressure is handled by ZeroMQ's high-water mark

docs.title: PUB Socket
docs.text:
1. Creates a PUB socket and binds/connects to the specified addresses
  2. All messages are broadcast to all connected SUB sockets
  3. Topic filtering happens at the subscriber side
  4. No persistence - messages to unconnected subscribers are dropped

docs.title: Pipeline (PUSH/PULL)
docs.text:
Distributes work across multiple workers:

[Fiddler PUSH] ──┬──► [PULL Worker 1]
                 ├──► [PULL Worker 2]
                 └──► [PULL Worker 3]

output:
  zeromq:
    socket_type: "push"
    bind: "tcp://*:5555"

docs.title: Pub/Sub
docs.text:
Broadcasts messages to all subscribers:

[Fiddler PUB] ───┬──► [SUB Client 1]
                 ├──► [SUB Client 2]
                 └──► [SUB Client 3]

output:
  zeromq:
    socket_type: "pub"
    bind: "tcp://*:5555"

docs.title: Work Distribution
docs.text:
input:
  http_server:
    port: 8080

processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let data = parse_json(this);
        data = set(data, "dispatched_at", now());
        this = bytes(str(data));

output:
  zeromq:
    socket_type: "push"
    bind: "tcp://*:5555"

Workers connect with PULL sockets to receive work.

docs.title: Event Broadcasting
docs.text:
input:
  kafka:
    brokers: ["kafka:9092"]
    topics: ["events"]

output:
  zeromq:
    socket_type: "pub"
    bind: "tcp://*:5556"

Any number of SUB clients can connect to receive all events.

docs.title: Push to Collector
docs.text:
input:
  mqtt:
    broker: "tcp://localhost:1883"
    topics:
      - "sensors/#"

output:
  zeromq:
    socket_type: "push"
    connect:
      - "tcp://collector:5555"

docs.title: Load Balancing Across Workers
docs.text:
input:
  amqp:
    url: "amqp://localhost"
    queue: "tasks"

output:
  zeromq:
    socket_type: "push"
    connect:
      - "tcp://worker1:5555"
      - "tcp://worker2:5555"
      - "tcp://worker3:5555"

Messages are distributed round-robin across workers.

docs.title: Bind vs Connect
docs.text:
Both PUSH and PUB sockets can either bind or connect:

# Producer binds, workers connect (common)
output:
  zeromq:
    socket_type: "push"
    bind: "tcp://*:5555"

# Workers bind, producer connects (reverse)
output:
  zeromq:
    socket_type: "push"
    connect:
      - "tcp://worker1:5555"
      - "tcp://worker2:5555"

docs.title: Topic Prefixing for Pub/Sub
docs.text:
Add topic prefixes to messages for subscriber filtering:

input:
  http_server:
    port: 8080

processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let data = parse_json(this);
        let topic = get(data, "type") ?? "default";
        // Prefix message with topic for subscriber filtering
        this = bytes(topic + ":" + str(data));

output:
  zeromq:
    socket_type: "pub"
    bind: "tcp://*:5555"

docs.title: Transport Types
docs.text: Transport Format Description TCP tcp://host:port Network communication IPC ipc:///path Inter-process (Unix sockets) inproc inproc://name In-process threads

docs.title: TCP Examples
docs.text:
bind: "tcp://*:5555"              # Bind to all interfaces
bind: "tcp://0.0.0.0:5555"        # Same as above
connect:
  - "tcp://remote:5555"           # Connect to remote host

docs.title: IPC Examples
docs.text:
bind: "ipc:///tmp/fiddler-out.sock"
connect:
  - "ipc:///tmp/collector.sock"

docs.title: PUSH vs PUB
docs.text: Feature PUSH PUB Delivery One receiver per message All subscribers Load balancing Yes (round-robin) No Slow receiver Back-pressure Messages dropped Use case Work distribution Broadcasting

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
• Bind failures: Error returned during startup
  • Connect failures: Logged, connection retried automatically
  • Send failures: Error returned to pipeline

docs.title: Performance Considerations
docs.text:
• High-water mark: ZeroMQ buffers messages; configure carefully for backpressure
  • Multiple connections: PUSH load-balances, PUB broadcasts to all
  • No persistence: Messages are ephemeral

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• zeromq input - Receive messages using ZeroMQ sockets
  • ZeroMQ Guide - Comprehensive ZeroMQ patterns

docs.title: Processors
docs.text:
Processors in fiddler handle all of the data transformation operations on the messages received from Inputs. Each Processor receives a single Message and is expected to return one to many messages as a result. Each result is forward along to processors later in the array; or to the desired output.

processors:
  - label: lines
    lines: {}
  - label: noop
    noop: {}
  - label: pretty print
    python:
      string: true
      code: |
        import json
        root = json.dumps(json.loads(root), indent=4)

docs.title: compress
docs.text:
Compress the message being processed

RequiredFull 
processors:
    - compress: {}

processors:
    - compress: 
        algorithm: Gzip

docs.title: algorithm
docs.text: The compression algorithm to use. [Default: Gzip] Type: string Required: false Accepted values: Gzip: compress the message using gzip. Zip: compress the message using deflate Zlib: compress the message using zlib

docs.title: decode
docs.text:
Decode the message

RequiredFull 
processors:
    - decode: {}

processors:
    - decode: 
        algorithm: Base64

docs.title: algorithm
docs.text: The decoding algorithm to use. [Default: Base64] Type: string Required: false Accepted values: Base64: decode the message as base64

docs.title: decompress
docs.text:
Decompress the message being processed

RequiredFull 
processors:
    - decompress: {}

processors:
    - decompress: 
        algorithm: Gzip

docs.title: algorithm
docs.text: The compression algorithm to use. [Default: Gzip] Type: string Required: false Accepted values: Gzip: decode the message as base64 Zip: decode the message as base64 Zlib: decode the message as base64

docs.title: fiddlerscript
docs.text:
FiddlerScript is the built-in scripting language for inline message manipulation in Fiddler. It provides a lightweight, C-style syntax for transforming messages without external dependencies.

BasicMultiple MessagesJSON ProcessingFilter Messages 
processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let text = bytes_to_string(this);
        this = bytes(text + " processed");

processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        // Split into multiple messages
        this = array(bytes("msg1"), bytes("msg2"), bytes("msg3"));

processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let data = parse_json(this);
        let name = get(data, "name");
        this = bytes(name);

processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let data = parse_json(this);
        let status = jmespath(data, "status");
        if (status == "ignore") {
            this = null;  // Filter out this message
        }

docs.title: code
docs.text: The FiddlerScript code to execute for each message. Type: string Required: true

docs.title: Variables
docs.text: The following variables are available in your script:

docs.title: this
docs.text:
The message content as bytes. This is the primary input and output variable.

  • Input: Contains the raw bytes of the incoming message
  • Output: Set this to the desired output bytes, or an array of bytes for multiple messages
// Read the message
let text = bytes_to_string(this);

// Modify and write back
this = bytes(text + " modified");

docs.title: metadata
docs.text:
A dictionary containing the message metadata. Keys are strings, values can be any type.

// Access metadata
let source = get(metadata, "source");
let timestamp = get(metadata, "timestamp");

// Use in processing
if (source == "important-queue") {
    this = bytes("PRIORITY: " + bytes_to_string(this));
}

docs.title: Returning Multiple Messages
docs.text:
To split a single input message into multiple output messages, set this to an array:

// Split message into multiple
this = array(bytes("first"), bytes("second"), bytes("third"));

Each element in the array becomes a separate message in the pipeline.

docs.title: Filtering Messages
docs.text:
To filter out (drop) a message from the pipeline, set this to null or an empty array:

// Filter using null
let data = parse_json(this);
if (jmespath(data, "skip") == true) {
    this = null;  // Message will be filtered
}

// Or use drop() function
if (should_filter) {
    this = drop(this);
}

// Or use empty array
if (no_output_needed) {
    this = array();  // Also filters the message
}

Filtered messages are tracked in the total_filtered metric and treated as successful completions.

docs.title: Data Conversion
docs.text: Function Description bytes(value) Convert any value to bytes bytes_to_string(bytes) Convert bytes to UTF-8 string str(value) Convert any value to string int(value) Convert to integer

docs.title: Collections
docs.text: Function Description array(items...) Create an array from arguments dict() Create an empty dictionary push(array, value) Add value to array (returns new array) get(collection, key) Get value by index/key set(collection, key, value) Set value (returns new collection) delete(collection, key) Remove element by index/key keys(dict) Get array of dictionary keys len(value) Get length of string/bytes/array/dict

docs.title: String Operations
docs.text: Function Description capitalize(str) Capitalize first character lowercase(str) Convert to lowercase uppercase(str) Convert to uppercase trim(str) Remove leading/trailing whitespace trim_prefix(str, prefix) Remove prefix if present trim_suffix(str, suffix) Remove suffix if present has_prefix(str, prefix) Check if starts with prefix has_suffix(str, suffix) Check if ends with suffix split(str, delim) Split by delimiter into array reverse(str) Reverse string lines(value) Split string/bytes into lines

docs.title: Compression
docs.text: Function Description gzip_compress(data) Compress with gzip gzip_decompress(data) Decompress gzip data zlib_compress(data) Compress with zlib zlib_decompress(data) Decompress zlib data deflate_compress(data) Compress with deflate deflate_decompress(data) Decompress deflate data

docs.title: Encoding
docs.text: Function Description base64_encode(data) Encode as base64 string base64_decode(data) Decode from base64 to bytes

docs.title: JSON
docs.text: Function Description parse_json(bytes) Parse JSON bytes into values jmespath(data, expr) Query data using JMESPath expressions

docs.title: Type Checking
docs.text: Function Description is_array(value) Check if value is an array is_dict(value) Check if value is a dictionary

docs.title: Environment
docs.text: Function Description getenv(name) Get environment variable (returns null if not set) print(values...) Print to stdout (for debugging) drop(value) Returns null (useful for filtering messages)

docs.title: Simple Transformation
docs.text:
processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let text = bytes_to_string(this);
        let upper = "";
        for (let i = 0; i < len(text); i = i + 1) {
            let c = get(text, i);
            // Simple uppercase (ASCII only)
            upper = upper + c;
        }
        this = bytes(upper);

docs.title: Filter by Content
docs.text:
processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let text = bytes_to_string(this);
        // Only keep messages containing "important"
        if (len(text) > 0) {
            this = bytes(text);
        } else {
            this = bytes("");  // Empty message will be processed
        }

docs.title: Parse and Extract JSON Field
docs.text:
processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let data = parse_json(this);
        let user = get(data, "user");
        let name = get(user, "name");
        let email = get(user, "email");
        this = bytes(name + " <" + email + ">");

docs.title: Extract Nested Fields with JMESPath
docs.text:
processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let data = parse_json(this);
        // Extract deeply nested field
        let email = jmespath(data, "user.profile.contact.email");
        // Extract all names from an array
        let names = jmespath(data, "users[*].name");
        this = bytes(str(names));

docs.title: Filter Messages by Content
docs.text:
processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let data = parse_json(this);
        let level = jmespath(data, "level");
        // Only keep error and warning messages
        if (level != "error" && level != "warning") {
            this = null;  // Filter out info/debug messages
        }

docs.title: Split JSON Array into Messages
docs.text:
processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let data = parse_json(this);
        let items = get(data, "items");
        let messages = array();
        for (let i = 0; i < len(items); i = i + 1) {
            let item = get(items, i);
            messages = push(messages, bytes(str(item)));
        }
        this = messages;

docs.title: Add Metadata to Message
docs.text:
processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let source = get(metadata, "source");
        let text = bytes_to_string(this);
        let enriched = "{\"source\": \"" + source + "\", \"data\": \"" + text + "\"}";
        this = bytes(enriched);

docs.title: Conditional Processing
docs.text:
processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let data = parse_json(this);
        let type = get(data, "type");

        if (type == "error") {
            // Add error prefix
            let msg = get(data, "message");
            this = bytes("ERROR: " + msg);
        } else if (type == "warning") {
            let msg = get(data, "message");
            this = bytes("WARN: " + msg);
        } else {
            // Pass through unchanged
        }

docs.title: Decompress and Process
docs.text:
processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        // Decompress gzip data
        let decompressed = gzip_decompress(this);
        let text = bytes_to_string(decompressed);

        // Process and recompress
        let processed = uppercase(text);
        this = gzip_compress(processed);

docs.title: Base64 Decode and Parse
docs.text:
processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        // Decode base64 payload
        let decoded = base64_decode(this);
        let json = parse_json(decoded);
        let message = get(json, "message");
        this = bytes(message);

docs.title: Split Lines into Messages
docs.text:
processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        // Split multi-line input into separate messages
        let all_lines = lines(this);
        let messages = array();
        for (let i = 0; i < len(all_lines); i = i + 1) {
            let line = get(all_lines, i);
            let trimmed = trim(line);
            if (len(trimmed) > 0) {
                messages = push(messages, bytes(trimmed));
            }
        }
        this = messages;

docs.title: String Manipulation
docs.text:
processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let text = bytes_to_string(this);

        // Remove prefix and normalize
        if (has_prefix(text, "LOG:")) {
            text = trim_prefix(text, "LOG:");
            text = trim(text);
            text = uppercase(text);
        }

        this = bytes(text);

docs.title: CSV Line Parsing
docs.text:
processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let text = bytes_to_string(this);
        let fields = split(text, ",");

        // Create JSON from CSV fields
        let json = "{";
        json = json + "\"name\": \"" + trim(get(fields, 0)) + "\", ";
        json = json + "\"value\": \"" + trim(get(fields, 1)) + "\"";
        json = json + "}";

        this = bytes(json);

docs.title: Language Reference
docs.text:
For complete FiddlerScript language documentation, see:

  • FiddlerScript Overview
  • Syntax Guide
  • Built-in Functions Reference

docs.title: Comparison with Python Processor
docs.text:
Feature FiddlerScript Python Dependencies None (built-in) Requires Python runtime Performance Fast (compiled) Slower (interpreted) Syntax C-style Python External libraries No Yes Variable name this root Compression gzip, zlib, deflate Any library Encoding Base64 Any encoding String methods Built-in Full Python

Use FiddlerScript for: - Simple to moderate transformations - JSON parsing and extraction - Message splitting - Compression/decompression (gzip, zlib, deflate) - Base64 encoding/decoding - String manipulation (trim, split, case conversion) - When you want no external dependencies

Use Python for: - Complex data processing - When you need external libraries - Regular expressions - Advanced numeric operations

docs.title: filter
docs.text:
Filter messages based on a JMESPath condition evaluated against JSON data. Messages that match the condition pass through; messages that don't match are dropped from the pipeline.

Basic

yml processors: - filter: condition: "status == 'active'"

Numeric Comparison

yml processors: - filter: condition: "age >= `18`"

Complex Condition

yml processors: - filter: condition: "type == 'order' && total > `100` && status != 'cancelled'"

docs.title: condition
docs.text:
A JMESPath expression that must evaluate to a boolean value. Messages where the condition evaluates to true pass through; messages where it evaluates to false are dropped.

Type: string Required: true

docs.title: label
docs.text:
Optional label for identifying this processor in logs and metrics.

Type: string Required: false

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. The processor parses the message bytes as JSON
  2. The JMESPath condition is evaluated against the JSON data
  3. If the result is true, the message continues through the pipeline
  4. If the result is false, the message is dropped (marked as a conditional check failure)
  5. If the condition doesn't return a boolean, a processing error occurs

docs.title: JMESPath Expressions
docs.text:
The filter processor uses JMESPath syntax for conditions. JMESPath is a query language for JSON that supports:

  • Field access: status, user.name, items[0]
  • Comparisons: ==, !=, <, <=, >, >=
  • Logical operators: &&, ||, !
  • Built-in functions: length(), contains(), starts_with(), ends_with(), etc.

docs.title: Literal Values
docs.text:
In JMESPath, literal values are enclosed in backticks:

  • Numbers: `100`, `3.14`
  • Booleans: `true`, `false`
  • Strings can use single quotes: 'active'
  • Null: `null`

docs.title: Filter by Field Value
docs.text:
Keep only messages with a specific status:

processors:
  - filter:
      condition: "status == 'active'"

docs.title: Filter by Numeric Range
docs.text:
Keep messages where the value is within a range:

processors:
  - filter:
      condition: "price >= `10` && price <= `100`"

docs.title: Filter by Nested Field
docs.text:
Access nested JSON fields:

processors:
  - filter:
      condition: "user.verified == `true`"

docs.title: Filter by Array Contents
docs.text:
Check if an array contains a specific value:

processors:
  - filter:
      condition: "contains(tags, 'important')"

docs.title: Filter by Array Length
docs.text:
Keep messages with non-empty arrays:

processors:
  - filter:
      condition: "length(items) > `0`"

docs.title: Filter by Null Check
docs.text:
Keep messages where a field is not null:

processors:
  - filter:
      condition: "error != null"

Or keep messages where a field is null:

processors:
  - filter:
      condition: "error == null"

docs.title: Filter by String Prefix
docs.text:
Keep messages where a field starts with a prefix:

processors:
  - filter:
      condition: "starts_with(name, 'prod-')"

docs.title: Complex Multi-Condition Filter
docs.text:
Combine multiple conditions:

processors:
  - filter:
      condition: "type == 'order' && total > `100` && status != 'cancelled'"

docs.title: Filter Log Levels
docs.text:
Keep only error and warning logs:

processors:
  - filter:
      condition: "level == 'error' || level == 'warning'"

docs.title: Filter by Type Check
docs.text:
Keep messages that have a specific field type:

processors:
  - filter:
      condition: "type(data) == 'object'"

docs.title: Invalid JSON
docs.text: If the message bytes are not valid JSON, a processing error is returned and the message is marked as failed.

docs.title: Non-Boolean Result
docs.text:
If the JMESPath expression doesn't return a boolean value, a processing error is returned:

# This will error because 'name' returns a string, not a boolean
processors:
  - filter:
      condition: "name" # Wrong - returns string value

Correct usage:

processors:
  - filter:
      condition: "name != null" # Correct - returns boolean

docs.title: Invalid JMESPath Expression
docs.text: If the JMESPath expression is invalid, an error is returned during configuration validation.

docs.title: Common JMESPath Functions
docs.text:
Function Description Example length(expr) Returns the length of a string, array, or object length(items) >0`` contains(arr, val) Check if array contains value contains(tags, 'important') starts_with(str, prefix) Check if string starts with prefix starts_with(name, 'prod-') ends_with(str, suffix) Check if string ends with suffix ends_with(file, '.json') type(expr) Returns the type of a value type(data) == 'array' not_null(expr...) Returns first non-null value not_null(name, 'unknown') to_string(expr) Convert to string - to_number(expr) Convert to number -

For the complete list of JMESPath functions, see the JMESPath specification.

docs.title: Comparison with Other Filtering Methods
docs.text: Method Use Case filter processor Simple boolean conditions on JSON fields switch processor Route messages to different processing paths based on conditions FiddlerScript this = null Complex filtering logic with full scripting capabilities

docs.title: Full Pipeline Example
docs.text:
label: Order Processing Pipeline
input:
  http_server:
    port: 8080
processors:
  # Only process valid orders
  - filter:
      condition: "type == 'order' && items != null && length(items) > `0`"
  # Additional processing...
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let data = parse_json(this);
        let total = jmespath(data, "sum(items[*].price)");
        // ... process order
output:
  stdout: {}

docs.title: lines
docs.text:
Split the received message into multiple messages separated by lines

Required 
processors:
    - lines: {}

docs.title: noop
docs.text:
performs no operation against the message

Required 
processors:
    - noop: {}

docs.title: python
docs.text:
Any third party packages utilized in Python will have to be installed on the OS running fiddler.

Required

yml processors: - python: code: | new_string = \"python rules\" root = new_string

Full

yml processors: - python: string: true code: | import json new_string = f\"python: {root}\" root = new_string

docs.title: string
docs.text: Indicate whether or not fiddler should convert the message to a string before invoking the python code. Type: string Required: false [default: false]

docs.title: code
docs.text: The python code to execute Type: string Required: true

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: The message is passed into the python code using a local variable root. The output taken from the executed code is also taken from the local variable root. By default root is bytes of the message, unless the argument string: true is proved, in which case root is converted to a string.

docs.title: switch
docs.text:
Switch accepts an array of valid processors and is expected to be utlized with the check output. Switch will attempt each provided processor; while failing to the next provided the error is returned is ConditionalCheckfailed. Other encountered errors will be surfaced.

Required 
processors:
  - switch: []

docs.title: check
docs.text:
Required 
processors:
  - switch:
      - check:
          condition: '\"Hello World\" > `5`'
          processors: 
            - noop: {}"

docs.title: condition
docs.text: Condition utilized for the execution of the processing step utilizing jmespath syntax for evaluation. As such, this can only be utilized with JSON documents. Type: string Required: true

docs.title: output
docs.text: Valid fiddler output module Type: object Required: true

docs.title: transform
docs.text:
Transform JSON messages by extracting fields using JMESPath expressions and mapping them to new field names. This processor creates a completely new JSON structure containing only the mapped fields.

BasicNested ExtractionArray Projection 
processors:
  - transform:
      mappings:
        - source: "name"
          target: "user_name"
        - source: "email"
          target: "user_email"

processors:
  - transform:
      mappings:
        - source: "user.profile.email"
          target: "email"
        - source: "user.name"
          target: "name"

processors:
  - transform:
      mappings:
        - source: "users[*].name"
          target: "all_names"

docs.title: mappings
docs.text:
An array of source-to-target field mappings. Each mapping extracts a value using a JMESPath expression and assigns it to a new field name.

Type: array Required: true

docs.title: Mapping Object
docs.text: Field Type Required Description source string Yes JMESPath expression to extract the value target string Yes Field name in the output JSON

docs.title: label
docs.text:
Optional label for identifying this processor in logs and metrics.

Type: string Required: false

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. The processor parses the input message bytes as JSON
  2. For each mapping, the JMESPath source expression is evaluated against the input
  3. The extracted value is assigned to the target field name
  4. A new JSON object is created containing only the mapped fields
  5. The original message structure is completely replaced

Important: The transform processor creates an entirely new JSON object. Fields not explicitly mapped are discarded.

docs.title: JMESPath Expressions
docs.text: The transform processor uses JMESPath syntax for source expressions. This enables powerful data extraction and transformation capabilities.

docs.title: Field Access
docs.text:
mappings:
  - source: "name"           # Top-level field
    target: "user_name"
  - source: "user.email"     # Nested field
    target: "email"
  - source: "items[0]"       # Array index
    target: "first_item"
  - source: "items[-1]"      # Negative index (last element)
    target: "last_item"

docs.title: Array Projections
docs.text:
Extract values from all elements in an array:

mappings:
  - source: "users[*].name"           # All names
    target: "names"
  - source: "orders[*].total"         # All totals
    target: "order_totals"

docs.title: Filtering
docs.text:
Filter array elements based on conditions:

mappings:
  - source: "items[?price > `100`]"           # Items over $100
    target: "expensive_items"
  - source: "users[?active == `true`].name"   # Active user names
    target: "active_users"

docs.title: Built-in Functions
docs.text:
Use JMESPath functions for transformations:

mappings:
  - source: "length(items)"           # Count items
    target: "item_count"
  - source: "join(', ', tags)"        # Join array to string
    target: "tags_string"
  - source: "sort(scores)"            # Sort array
    target: "sorted_scores"

docs.title: Multiselect
docs.text:
Create nested objects in a single mapping:

mappings:
  - source: "{full_name: name, user_email: email}"
    target: "contact"

This produces: 

{
  "contact": {
    "full_name": "Alice",
    "user_email": "alice@example.com"
  }
}

docs.title: Rename Fields
docs.text:
Rename fields while keeping the same values:

processors:
  - transform:
      mappings:
        - source: "firstName"
          target: "first_name"
        - source: "lastName"
          target: "last_name"
        - source: "emailAddress"
          target: "email"

Input: 

{"firstName": "Alice", "lastName": "Smith", "emailAddress": "alice@example.com"}

Output: 

{"first_name": "Alice", "last_name": "Smith", "email": "alice@example.com"}

docs.title: Flatten Nested Structure
docs.text:
Extract nested fields to a flat structure:

processors:
  - transform:
      mappings:
        - source: "user.profile.name"
          target: "name"
        - source: "user.profile.contact.email"
          target: "email"
        - source: "user.settings.theme"
          target: "theme"

Input: 

{
  "user": {
    "profile": {
      "name": "Bob",
      "contact": {"email": "bob@example.com"}
    },
    "settings": {"theme": "dark"}
  }
}

Output: 

{"name": "Bob", "email": "bob@example.com", "theme": "dark"}

docs.title: Extract Array Elements
docs.text:
Extract specific elements from arrays:

processors:
  - transform:
      mappings:
        - source: "items[0]"
          target: "first"
        - source: "items[-1]"
          target: "last"
        - source: "items[1:3]"
          target: "middle"

docs.title: Project Array Fields
docs.text:
Extract a specific field from all objects in an array:

processors:
  - transform:
      mappings:
        - source: "orders[*].id"
          target: "order_ids"
        - source: "orders[*].total"
          target: "totals"

Input: 

{
  "orders": [
    {"id": "A1", "total": 100},
    {"id": "A2", "total": 200}
  ]
}

Output: 

{"order_ids": ["A1", "A2"], "totals": [100, 200]}

docs.title: Compute Aggregates
docs.text:
Use JMESPath functions for calculations:

processors:
  - transform:
      mappings:
        - source: "length(items)"
          target: "count"
        - source: "sum(items[*].price)"
          target: "total_price"
        - source: "max(items[*].price)"
          target: "max_price"
        - source: "min(items[*].price)"
          target: "min_price"

docs.title: Filter and Transform
docs.text:
Filter array elements and extract specific fields:

processors:
  - transform:
      mappings:
        - source: "products[?inStock == `true`].name"
          target: "available_products"
        - source: "products[?price < `50`] | length(@)"
          target: "affordable_count"

docs.title: Create Nested Output
docs.text:
Build nested structures using multiselect:

processors:
  - transform:
      mappings:
        - source: "{name: user.name, email: user.email}"
          target: "contact"
        - source: "{city: address.city, country: address.country}"
          target: "location"

Output: 

{
  "contact": {"name": "Alice", "email": "alice@example.com"},
  "location": {"city": "NYC", "country": "USA"}
}

docs.title: Handle Missing Fields
docs.text:
Missing fields result in null values:

processors:
  - transform:
      mappings:
        - source: "required_field"
          target: "required"
        - source: "optional_field"
          target: "optional"

If optional_field doesn't exist, the output will be: 

{"required": "value", "optional": null}

docs.title: Invalid JSON
docs.text: If the message bytes are not valid JSON, a processing error is returned.

docs.title: Invalid JMESPath Expression
docs.text: Invalid JMESPath expressions are caught during configuration validation, preventing startup with malformed expressions.

docs.title: Common JMESPath Functions
docs.text:
Function Description Example length(expr) Array/string/object length length(items) sum(array) Sum of numbers sum(prices) avg(array) Average of numbers avg(scores) min(array) Minimum value min(prices) max(array) Maximum value max(prices) sort(array) Sort array sort(names) reverse(array) Reverse array reverse(items) join(sep, array) Join strings join(', ', tags) keys(object) Get object keys keys(data) values(object) Get object values values(data) contains(arr, val) Check membership contains(tags, 'urgent') starts_with(str, prefix) String prefix check starts_with(name, 'prod') ends_with(str, suffix) String suffix check ends_with(file, '.json') to_string(expr) Convert to string to_string(count) to_number(expr) Convert to number to_number(value)

For the complete list, see the JMESPath specification.

docs.title: Comparison with Other Processors
docs.text: Processor Use Case transform Restructure JSON with field extraction and renaming filter Keep or drop entire messages based on conditions fiddlerscript Complex transformations with full scripting

docs.title: Preserving Original Fields
docs.text:
The transform processor creates a new structure, discarding unmapped fields. To keep original fields alongside new ones, use the fiddlerscript processor instead:

processors:
  - fiddlerscript:
      code: |
        let data = parse_json(this);
        // Add new field while keeping original
        data = set(data, "full_name", get(data, "first") + " " + get(data, "last"));
        this = bytes(str(data));

docs.title: Full Pipeline Example
docs.text:
label: Order Processing
input:
  http_server:
    port: 8080
processors:
  # Extract and restructure order data
  - transform:
      mappings:
        - source: "order.id"
          target: "order_id"
        - source: "order.items[*].sku"
          target: "skus"
        - source: "sum(order.items[*].price)"
          target: "total"
        - source: "customer.email"
          target: "email"
        - source: "length(order.items)"
          target: "item_count"
output:
  stdout: {}

Input: 

{
  "order": {
    "id": "ORD-123",
    "items": [
      {"sku": "PROD-A", "price": 29.99},
      {"sku": "PROD-B", "price": 49.99}
    ]
  },
  "customer": {
    "name": "Alice",
    "email": "alice@example.com"
  }
}

Output: 

{
  "order_id": "ORD-123",
  "skus": ["PROD-A", "PROD-B"],
  "total": 79.98,
  "email": "alice@example.com",
  "item_count": 2
}

docs.title: try
docs.text:
Attempt to run a given processor and either continue processing or try alternative processors upon failure

RequiredFull 
processors:
    - try: 
        processor: 
          noop: {}

processors:
    - try: 
        processor: 
          noop: {}
        catch:
          - noop: {}

docs.title: processor
docs.text: The fiddler processor to use Type: object Required: true

docs.title: catch
docs.text: An array of fiddler processors to run if the initial processor fails Type: array Required: false

docs.title: FiddlerScript
docs.text: FiddlerScript is a minimal C-style scripting language with a Rust-based interpreter, designed for inline message manipulation within Fiddler pipelines.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
FiddlerScript provides a lightweight alternative to Python for simple data transformations. It supports:

  • Data Types: integers, strings, booleans, bytes, arrays, and dictionaries
  • Literal Syntax: array literals [1, 2, 3] and dictionary literals {"key": value}
  • Control Flow: if-else statements and for loops
  • Functions: user-defined functions with recursion support
  • Built-ins: print(), len(), str(), int(), getenv(), parse_json(), compression, base64, and more
  • Comments: single-line comments with //

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
// Process a JSON message
let data = parse_json(this);
let name = get(data, "name");

if (name) {
    print("Processing user:", name);
} else {
    print("Unknown user");
}

// Create a response
let response = {"status": "ok", "processed": true};
this = bytes(response);

// Split message into multiple outputs
let lines = lines(this);
let results = [];
for (let i = 0; i < len(lines); i = i + 1) {
    let line = get(lines, i);
    if (len(line) > 0) {
        results = push(results, bytes(line));
    }
}
this = results;

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
By default, FiddlerScript loads all OS environment variables as script variables, making them directly accessible:

// Access environment variables directly
print(HOME);
print(USER);

// Or use getenv() for dynamic access
let key = "PATH";
print(getenv(key));

docs.title: Integration with Fiddler
docs.text:
FiddlerScript is designed to integrate seamlessly with Fiddler's message processing pipeline. Variables can be injected from Rust code before script execution, and results can be retrieved after execution.

use fiddler_script::{Interpreter, Value};

let mut interpreter = Interpreter::new();

// Inject message data
interpreter.set_variable_bytes("message", msg_bytes);
interpreter.set_variable_string("source", "input-queue");

// Run the script
interpreter.run(script_source)?;

// Retrieve results
if let Some(result) = interpreter.get_value("output") {
    // Process the result
}

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
• Syntax Guide - Language syntax and constructs
  • Built-in Functions - Reference for all built-in functions
  • Integration Guide - Using FiddlerScript from Rust

docs.title: Built-in Functions
docs.text:
FiddlerScript provides a set of built-in functions for common operations.

Note: Most built-in functions can be called using either function syntax func(value, args) or method syntax value.func(args). Both are equivalent. Method syntax is useful for chaining operations.

docs.title: print(args...)
docs.text:
Print values to stdout.

Arguments: Any number of values Returns: null

print("Hello");                    // Hello
print("Count:", 42);               // Count: 42
print("a", "b", "c");              // a b c

docs.title: len(value) / value.len()
docs.text:
Get the length of a string, bytes, array, or dictionary.

Arguments: A string, bytes, array, or dictionary value Returns: Integer (length)

// Function syntax
let s = "hello";
print(len(s));          // 5

// Method syntax
print(s.len());         // 5
print([1, 2, 3].len()); // 3

docs.title: str(value)
docs.text:
Convert any value to a string.

Arguments: Any value Returns: String representation

let n = 42;
let s = str(n);         // "42"

let b = true;
let bs = str(b);        // "true"

docs.title: bytes(value)
docs.text:
Convert any value to bytes.

Arguments: Any value Returns: Bytes representation

let b = bytes("hello");     // String to bytes
let n = bytes(42);          // "42" as bytes
let f = bytes(true);        // "true" as bytes

docs.title: bytes_to_string(bytes) / bytes.bytes_to_string()
docs.text:
Convert bytes to a UTF-8 string.

Arguments: Bytes or string value Returns: String

// Function syntax
let text = bytes_to_string(data);

// Method syntax
let text = data.bytes_to_string();

Invalid UTF-8 sequences are replaced with the Unicode replacement character.

docs.title: int(value)
docs.text:
Convert a value to an integer.

Arguments: String, integer, float, boolean, bytes, or null Returns: Integer

let n = int("42");      // 42
let b = int(true);      // 1
let f = int(false);     // 0
let z = int(null);      // 0
let t = int(3.99);      // 3 (truncates float)
let neg = int(-2.7);    // -2 (truncates toward zero)

Strings must contain valid integer representations, or a runtime error occurs.

docs.title: float(value)
docs.text:
Convert a value to a float.

Arguments: String, integer, float, or boolean Returns: Float

let f = float(42);          // 42.0
let pi = float("3.14159");  // 3.14159
let one = float(true);      // 1.0
let zero = float(false);    // 0.0

docs.title: getenv(name)
docs.text:
Get an environment variable by name.

Arguments: String (variable name) Returns: String value or null if not found

let home = getenv("HOME");
if (home) {
    print("Home directory:", home);
} else {
    print("HOME not set");
}

Note: Environment variables are also loaded as script variables by default, so you can access them directly:

print(HOME);      // Same as getenv("HOME")
print(PATH);      // Same as getenv("PATH")

docs.title: drop(value)
docs.text:
Returns null. This is a convenience function for clearing variables or signaling that a value should be discarded.

Arguments: Any value (the value is ignored) Returns: null

let data = "temporary";
data = drop(data);        // data is now null

// Useful for clearing variables
let temp = some_computation();
// ... use temp ...
temp = drop(temp);        // Clear temp

Note: In the context of the FiddlerScript processor, setting this to null (either directly or via drop()) will filter out the message from the pipeline.

docs.title: parse_json(data)
docs.text:
Parse JSON from bytes or a string.

Arguments: Bytes or string containing valid JSON Returns: Parsed value (dictionary, array, string, integer, boolean, or null)

// Parse a JSON string
let s = parse_json("\"hello\"");      // "hello"

// Parse a JSON number
let n = parse_json("42");             // 42

// Parse a JSON boolean
let b = parse_json("true");           // true

// Parse a JSON null
let z = parse_json("null");           // null

// Parse a JSON object (returns dictionary)
let obj = parse_json("{\"name\": \"Alice\", \"age\": 30}");
let name = get(obj, "name");          // "Alice"

// Parse a JSON array (returns array)
let arr = parse_json("[1, 2, 3]");
let first = get(arr, 0);              // 1

Supported JSON types: - null -> null - true/false -> boolean - numbers -> integer (floats are truncated) - strings -> string - arrays -> array - objects -> dictionary

docs.title: jmespath(data, expression)
docs.text:
Query JSON data using JMESPath expressions. JMESPath is a query language for JSON that allows you to extract and transform elements from a JSON document.

Arguments: - data - A dictionary or array (typically from parse_json()) - expression - A JMESPath expression string

Returns: The extracted value, or null if not found

// Simple key access
let data = parse_json("{\"name\": \"Alice\", \"age\": 30}");
let name = jmespath(data, "name");           // "Alice"

// Nested key access
let nested = parse_json("{\"user\": {\"profile\": {\"email\": \"alice@example.com\"}}}");
let email = jmespath(nested, "user.profile.email");  // "alice@example.com"

// Array indexing
let arr = parse_json("[\"a\", \"b\", \"c\"]");
let second = jmespath(arr, "[1]");           // "b"

// Array projections
let users = parse_json("[{\"name\": \"Alice\"}, {\"name\": \"Bob\"}]");
let names = jmespath(users, "[*].name");     // ["Alice", "Bob"]

// Filtering
let items = parse_json("[{\"price\": 10}, {\"price\": 25}, {\"price\": 5}]");
let expensive = jmespath(items, "[?price > `15`]");  // [{"price": 25}]

Common JMESPath expressions: - key - Get a key from an object - key1.key2 - Nested key access - [0] - Array index - [*] - All array elements - [*].key - Project key from all array elements - [?condition] - Filter array elements - key1 || key2 - Return first non-null value

docs.title: array(args...)
docs.text:
Create a new array from arguments.

Arguments: Any number of values Returns: Array containing all arguments

let arr = array(1, 2, 3);
let mixed = array("hello", 42, true);
let empty = array();

docs.title: push(array, value)
docs.text:
Add a value to the end of an array.

Arguments: An array, a value to add Returns: A new array with the value appended

let arr = array(1, 2);
let arr2 = push(arr, 3);  // array(1, 2, 3)

docs.title: get(collection, index)
docs.text:
Get a value from an array, dictionary, or string.

Arguments: - For arrays: array, integer index - For strings: string, integer index - For dictionaries: dictionary, string key

Returns: The value at the index/key, or null if not found

let arr = array("a", "b", "c");
let first = get(arr, 0);       // "a"

let s = "hello";
let char = get(s, 1);          // "e"

let d = set(dict(), "key", "value");
let v = get(d, "key");         // "value"

docs.title: set(collection, index, value)
docs.text:
Set a value in an array or dictionary.

Arguments: - For arrays: array, integer index, value - For dictionaries: dictionary, string key, value

Returns: A new array/dictionary with the value set

let arr = array(1, 2, 3);
let arr2 = set(arr, 1, 99);  // array(1, 99, 3)

let d = dict();
let d2 = set(d, "name", "Alice");

docs.title: is_array(value)
docs.text:
Check if a value is an array.

Arguments: Any value Returns: Boolean

let arr = array(1, 2);
let check = is_array(arr);  // true
let check2 = is_array(42);  // false

docs.title: dict()
docs.text:
Create an empty dictionary.

Arguments: None Returns: Empty dictionary

let d = dict();
let d2 = set(d, "key", "value");

docs.title: keys(dictionary)
docs.text:
Get all keys from a dictionary.

Arguments: A dictionary Returns: Array of keys (strings) in insertion order

let d = set(set(dict(), "a", 1), "b", 2);
let k = keys(d);  // array("a", "b") - order is preserved

Note: Keys are returned in the order they were inserted into the dictionary.

docs.title: is_dict(value)
docs.text:
Check if a value is a dictionary.

Arguments: Any value Returns: Boolean

let d = dict();
let check = is_dict(d);     // true
let check2 = is_dict(42);   // false

docs.title: delete(collection, key)
docs.text:
Remove an element from a dictionary or array.

Arguments: - For dictionaries: dictionary, string key - For arrays: array, integer index

Returns: A new collection with the element removed

let d = set(dict(), "name", "Alice");
let d2 = delete(d, "name");  // Empty dictionary

let arr = array(1, 2, 3);
let arr2 = delete(arr, 1);   // array(1, 3)

docs.title: contains(collection, item) / collection.contains(item)
docs.text:
Check if a collection contains a value or key.

Arguments: - For arrays: array, value to search for - For dictionaries: dictionary, string key to check

Returns: Boolean (true if found, false otherwise)

// Array examples
let numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];
numbers.contains(3);           // true
numbers.contains(10);          // false
contains(numbers, 2);          // true (function syntax)

let fruits = ["apple", "banana"];
fruits.contains("banana");     // true

// Dictionary examples
let user = {"name": "Alice", "age": 30};
user.contains("name");         // true
user.contains("email");        // false
contains(user, "age");         // true (function syntax)

// In conditionals
if (users.contains("admin")) {
    print("Admin exists");
}

docs.title: String Operations
docs.text: All string operations support both function and method syntax.

docs.title: capitalize(string) / string.capitalize()
docs.text:
Capitalize the first character of a string.

Arguments: String Returns: String with first character uppercased

let s = "hello".capitalize();  // "Hello"
let s2 = capitalize("HELLO");  // "HELLO"

docs.title: lowercase(string) / string.lowercase()
docs.text:
Convert a string to lowercase.

Arguments: String Returns: Lowercase string

let s = "Hello World".lowercase();  // "hello world"

docs.title: uppercase(string) / string.uppercase()
docs.text:
Convert a string to uppercase.

Arguments: String Returns: Uppercase string

let s = "Hello World".uppercase();  // "HELLO WORLD"

docs.title: trim(string) / string.trim()
docs.text:
Remove leading and trailing whitespace from a string.

Arguments: String Returns: Trimmed string

let s = "  hello  ".trim();  // "hello"

docs.title: trim_prefix(string, prefix)
docs.text:
Remove a prefix from a string if present.

Arguments: String, prefix string Returns: String with prefix removed (or original if no match)

let s = trim_prefix("hello world", "hello ");  // "world"
let s2 = trim_prefix("hello", "bye");          // "hello"

docs.title: trim_suffix(string, suffix)
docs.text:
Remove a suffix from a string if present.

Arguments: String, suffix string Returns: String with suffix removed (or original if no match)

let s = trim_suffix("hello.txt", ".txt");  // "hello"
let s2 = trim_suffix("hello", ".txt");     // "hello"

docs.title: has_prefix(string, prefix)
docs.text:
Check if a string starts with a prefix.

Arguments: String, prefix string Returns: Boolean

let check = has_prefix("hello world", "hello");  // true
let check2 = has_prefix("hello", "bye");         // false

docs.title: has_suffix(string, suffix)
docs.text:
Check if a string ends with a suffix.

Arguments: String, suffix string Returns: Boolean

let check = has_suffix("hello.txt", ".txt");  // true
let check2 = has_suffix("hello", ".txt");     // false

docs.title: split(string, delimiter) / string.split(delimiter)
docs.text:
Split a string by a delimiter.

Arguments: String, delimiter string Returns: Array of strings

let parts = "a,b,c".split(",");  // ["a", "b", "c"]
let words = "hello world".split(" ");  // ["hello", "world"]

docs.title: reverse(string)
docs.text:
Reverse a string.

Arguments: String Returns: Reversed string

let s = reverse("hello");  // "olleh"

docs.title: lines(value)
docs.text:
Split a string or bytes into lines.

Arguments: String or bytes Returns: Array of strings (one per line)

let text = "line1
line2
line3";
let arr = lines(text);  // array("line1", "line2", "line3")

Handles both Unix ( ) and Windows ( ) line endings.

docs.title: gzip_compress(data, level?)
docs.text:
Compress data using gzip.

Arguments: - data - String or bytes to compress - level (optional) - Compression level 0-9 (default: 6). 0 = no compression, 9 = maximum compression.

Returns: Compressed bytes

let compressed = gzip_compress("hello world");
let max_compressed = gzip_compress("hello world", 9);  // Maximum compression
let fast_compressed = gzip_compress("hello world", 1); // Fast compression

docs.title: gzip_decompress(data)
docs.text:
Decompress gzip-compressed data.

Arguments: Bytes Returns: Decompressed bytes

let original = gzip_decompress(compressed);
let text = bytes_to_string(original);

docs.title: zlib_compress(data, level?)
docs.text:
Compress data using zlib.

Arguments: - data - String or bytes to compress - level (optional) - Compression level 0-9 (default: 6). 0 = no compression, 9 = maximum compression.

Returns: Compressed bytes

let compressed = zlib_compress("hello world");
let max_compressed = zlib_compress("hello world", 9);  // Maximum compression

docs.title: zlib_decompress(data)
docs.text:
Decompress zlib-compressed data.

Arguments: Bytes Returns: Decompressed bytes

let original = zlib_decompress(compressed);

docs.title: deflate_compress(data, level?)
docs.text:
Compress data using raw deflate.

Arguments: - data - String or bytes to compress - level (optional) - Compression level 0-9 (default: 6). 0 = no compression, 9 = maximum compression.

Returns: Compressed bytes

let compressed = deflate_compress("hello world");
let max_compressed = deflate_compress("hello world", 9);  // Maximum compression

docs.title: deflate_decompress(data)
docs.text:
Decompress raw deflate-compressed data.

Arguments: Bytes Returns: Decompressed bytes

let original = deflate_decompress(compressed);

docs.title: base64_encode(data)
docs.text:
Encode data as base64.

Arguments: String or bytes Returns: Base64-encoded string

let encoded = base64_encode("hello");  // "aGVsbG8="

docs.title: base64_decode(data)
docs.text:
Decode base64-encoded data.

Arguments: String or bytes Returns: Decoded bytes

let decoded = base64_decode("aGVsbG8=");
let text = bytes_to_string(decoded);  // "hello"

docs.title: abs(value) / value.abs()
docs.text:
Get the absolute value of a number.

Arguments: Integer or float Returns: Same type as input (absolute value)

abs(-42);          // 42
abs(-3.14);        // 3.14

let x = -10;
x.abs();           // 10 (method syntax)

let y = -2.5;
y.abs();           // 2.5 (method syntax)

docs.title: ceil(value) / value.ceil()
docs.text:
Ceiling function - rounds up to the nearest integer.

Arguments: Integer or float Returns: Integer

ceil(42);          // 42 (integers unchanged)
ceil(3.14);        // 4
ceil(-3.14);       // -3

let x = 2.5;
x.ceil();          // 3 (method syntax)

docs.title: floor(value) / value.floor()
docs.text:
Floor function - rounds down to the nearest integer.

Arguments: Integer or float Returns: Integer

floor(42);         // 42 (integers unchanged)
floor(3.99);       // 3
floor(-3.14);      // -4

let x = 2.5;
x.floor();         // 2 (method syntax)

docs.title: round(value) / value.round()
docs.text:
Round function - rounds to the nearest integer.

Arguments: Integer or float Returns: Integer

round(42);         // 42 (integers unchanged)
round(3.4);        // 3
round(3.5);        // 4
round(-3.5);       // -4

let x = 2.5;
x.round();         // 3 (method syntax)

docs.title: timestamp()
docs.text:
Get the current Unix timestamp in seconds.

Arguments: None Returns: Integer (seconds since Unix epoch)

let now = timestamp();
print("Current time:", now);  // e.g., 1706284800

docs.title: epoch()
docs.text:
Alias for timestamp(). Get the current Unix timestamp in seconds.

Arguments: None Returns: Integer (seconds since Unix epoch)

let now = epoch();

docs.title: timestamp_millis()
docs.text:
Get the current Unix timestamp in milliseconds.

Arguments: None Returns: Integer (milliseconds since Unix epoch)

let now_ms = timestamp_millis();
print("Milliseconds:", now_ms);  // e.g., 1706284800123

// Measure elapsed time
let start = timestamp_millis();
// ... do work ...
let elapsed = timestamp_millis() - start;
print("Took", elapsed, "ms");

docs.title: timestamp_micros()
docs.text:
Get the current Unix timestamp in microseconds.

Arguments: None Returns: Integer (microseconds since Unix epoch)

let now_us = timestamp_micros();
print("Microseconds:", now_us);  // e.g., 1706284800123456

docs.title: timestamp_iso8601()
docs.text:
Get the current time as an ISO 8601 formatted string (RFC 3339).

Arguments: None Returns: String in ISO 8601 format

let now = timestamp_iso8601();
print("Current time:", now);  // e.g., "2024-01-26T12:34:56.789+00:00"

// Use in logging
fn log(message) {
    let ts = timestamp_iso8601();
    print("[" + ts + "]", message);
}

log("Application started");
